q: A researcher induces a recessive loss-of-function mutation in a single gene of a bacterium and immediately observes the mutant phenotype in the very first colony that grows from the treated cell. In a diploid organism, the same fresh mutation would typically be masked. Which feature of bacteria best explains why the phenotype appears right away?
options: The bacterial genome is haploid, so there is no second allele to mask the mutation
Bacteria repair recessive mutations faster than they express them
Bacteria reproduce sexually, shuffling the mutation into the open
The mutation is automatically converted to a dominant form during replication
explain: Because a bacterium carries only one copy of most genes, a new recessive mutation has no homologous allele to cover for it, so the altered phenotype is expressed in the same cell. In a diploid, a normal allele on the homolog usually masks a fresh recessive change. Faster repair would hide, not reveal, the phenotype; bacteria divide asexually rather than sexually; and mutations are not spontaneously made dominant by replication.
hint: Think about how many copies of each gene the cell carries and whether anything could cover for the change.

q: Two microbiology students debate why bacteria and viruses became central tools in early genetics. One claims it is mainly because their genomes are large and complex, mirroring human genetics. Why is this claim incorrect?
options: Bacterial and viral genomes are actually relatively small, which simplifies analysis rather than complicates it
Their genomes are large, but the complexity is what made them useful
Genome size is irrelevant; only generation time mattered to early geneticists
Viruses have no genome, so size cannot be a factor at all
explain: A key practical advantage of these organisms is that their genomes are comparatively small and compact, which makes mutations, mapping, and sequencing more tractable, not more complex. The student's premise is backwards. Generation time matters too, but the statement that size is irrelevant is false, and viruses certainly do possess a genome.
hint: Reconsider whether the genomes of these organisms are big and complicated or compact and streamlined.

q: A lab starts with one bacterial cell and lets it grow overnight, producing billions of descendants without any genetic exchange between cells. The researcher wants a population that is genetically uniform for a downstream experiment. Why does this asexual growth produce exactly what she needs?
options: Asexual binary fission yields a clonal population, so all cells share the same genotype barring rare new mutations
Asexual growth randomly reassorts genes, generating uniform diversity
Each division creates a new species, ensuring purity
Sexual recombination during growth homogenizes the genotypes
explain: Binary fission copies the chromosome and partitions it, so every descendant inherits the same genotype as the founder, giving a pure clonal strain except for occasional new mutations. Asexual division does not reassort or recombine genes, it does not create new species, and there is no sexual recombination occurring here.
hint: Ask what kind of population you get when one cell copies itself over and over with no mixing of DNA.

q: On a global scale, marine microorganisms have an outsized impact on the atmosphere. Which statement most accurately captures the contribution of ocean-dwelling photosynthetic bacteria?
options: They generate roughly half of the planet's oxygen while consuming carbon dioxide
They produce nearly all atmospheric nitrogen and release oxygen
They consume oxygen and release the bulk of atmospheric carbon dioxide
They have negligible effect because they live too deep to photosynthesize
explain: Photosynthetic ocean bacteria are responsible for producing on the order of half of Earth's oxygen and they take up carbon dioxide in the process. They are not the source of atmospheric nitrogen, they do not net-consume oxygen, and they live in sunlit surface waters where photosynthesis occurs, so their effect is large rather than negligible.
hint: Recall which gas these organisms release and which they take in, and just how large a share of the global supply that represents.

q: A textbook states that archaea are simply a kind of bacteria restricted to boiling springs and salt flats. A genomics study challenges this. Which finding from comparative genomics most directly refutes the textbook's framing?
options: Archaea are genetically distinct from bacteria and, in many core systems, more closely related to eukaryotes, and they also inhabit ordinary environments
Archaea share nearly identical genomes with bacteria and live only in extremes
Archaea are a subgroup of eukaryotes that lost their nucleus
Archaea cannot survive outside high-temperature habitats
explain: Sequence comparisons show archaea form a lineage distinct from eubacteria and share features of their information-processing machinery with eukaryotes, and they are found in moderate as well as extreme habitats. They are not genetically near-identical to bacteria, they are not nucleus-lacking eukaryotes, and they are not confined to extreme environments.
hint: Consider both where archaea actually live and which domain their core molecular machinery resembles.

q: During cell division, a bacterium assembles a ring of FtsZ protein at the future division site that constricts to pinch the cell in two. FtsZ is frequently cited as evidence that prokaryotes are more structurally sophisticated than once assumed. What property of FtsZ supports this view?
options: It is structurally and evolutionarily related to eukaryotic tubulin, a cytoskeletal protein
It is identical to eukaryotic DNA polymerase and copies the chromosome
It is a lipid that simply dissolves the membrane at the midpoint
It is unique to prokaryotes with no counterpart anywhere in eukaryotes
explain: FtsZ shares its GTP-binding fold, GTPase activity, and protofilament-forming behavior with tubulin, the building block of eukaryotic microtubules, revealing that bacteria possess cytoskeleton-like machinery once thought absent in prokaryotes. It is not a DNA polymerase, it is a protein rather than a lipid and acts by forming a contractile ring rather than dissolving membrane, and far from being counterpart-free, its eukaryotic relative is precisely what makes it noteworthy.
hint: This division-ring protein is a structural and evolutionary cousin of the eukaryotic protein that builds microtubules.

q: A student wants to estimate the total number of biological particles in a liter of seawater. A microbial ecologist tells her that, by sheer count, one category of biological entity dominates not just the ocean but the entire biosphere. Which is it, and why does this matter for genetics?
options: Viruses, the most abundant biological entities on Earth, making them a vast reservoir of genetic material and exchange
Large multicellular animals, whose abundance drives most gene flow
Archaea, which outnumber all viruses and bacteria combined in every habitat
Fungi, which form the largest share of biological particles in water
explain: Viruses are the most numerous biological entities on the planet, vastly outnumbering cellular life, which makes them an enormous pool of genetic information and a major conduit for moving genes between hosts. Multicellular animals are comparatively rare in particle counts, archaea do not outnumber all viruses, and fungi are not the dominant particle in seawater.
hint: Think about which non-cellular agent vastly outnumbers all cellular organisms when you count particles.

q: A microbiologist streaks a soil bacterium onto a plate of minimal medium (containing only inorganic salts, a carbon source, and water) and obtains healthy colonies after incubation. What can be concluded about this strain?
options: It is a prototroph capable of synthesizing all the organic compounds it requires for growth
It is an auxotroph that depends on supplements supplied by the plate
It must carry a plasmid encoding the biosynthetic enzymes it lacks
It can grow only if amino acids and vitamins are added to the medium
explain: Minimal medium supplies only salts, a carbon source, and water, so any cell that forms colonies on it must be able to manufacture every amino acid, vitamin, and other organic building block on its own. That self-sufficiency is the defining feature of a wild-type prototroph. A strain needing supplements could not grow on such a bare medium, so describing it as supplement-dependent contradicts the observed growth. No extra genetic element is required for biosynthesis; the chromosomal genes suffice, so invoking a plasmid is unwarranted. Requiring added amino acids and vitamins describes a deficient strain, which is the opposite of what the result shows.
hint: Ask what a cell must be able to make for itself if the plate provides nothing but salts, carbon, and water.

q: In a replica-plating experiment, a master plate of mutagenized bacteria is grown on medium supplemented with histidine, then velvet-transferred to two plates: one containing histidine and one lacking it. A particular colony appears on the histidine-containing plate but is absent at the corresponding position on the histidine-free plate. What does this pattern indicate about that colony?
options: It is a histidine auxotroph that cannot grow without an external histidine supply
It is a prototroph that overproduces histidine
It has lost its chromosome and survives only on rich medium
It is a contaminant fungus rather than a bacterial clone
explain: Replica plating compares growth with and without a single nutrient at matched positions. A colony that grows only when histidine is present has lost the ability to make histidine and must obtain it from the medium, which is exactly an auxotroph for that amino acid. A prototroph would grow in both conditions because it synthesizes its own histidine, so absence on the unsupplemented plate rules that out. Cells cannot survive without their chromosome, making chromosome loss biologically impossible. The growth-only-with-supplement pattern is the signature of a defined biosynthetic defect, not evidence of a foreign organism, which would not show this specific nutritional dependence.
hint: Focus on which single condition allows growth and what biosynthetic capability that difference reveals.

q: Which statement most accurately describes how the protein-coding content of the E. coli chromosome compares with that of the human genome?
options: A much larger fraction of the E. coli genome codes for protein than in humans, where only a small percentage does
Both genomes devote roughly equal fractions of their DNA to protein-coding sequence
The human genome is almost entirely coding, whereas E. coli DNA is mostly noncoding
Neither genome contains appreciable amounts of protein-coding DNA
explain: In E. coli the great majority of the chromosome encodes protein, leaving little intervening sequence, whereas in humans only a tiny fraction of the genome is protein-coding and the rest is noncoding. Claiming the fractions are equal ignores this striking contrast in genome economy. Reversing the comparison, with humans dense and bacteria sparse, is the opposite of reality. Saying neither genome codes for protein is simply false, since both clearly contain genes that specify proteins.
hint: Think about what proportion of each genome is actually coding versus noncoding, and whether that proportion is the same in a small prokaryote and a large eukaryote.

q: A plasmid carrying a kanamycin-resistance gene is introduced into a bacterial cell. Even though the host chromosome's replication is experimentally halted at a certain temperature, the plasmid continues to be copied and passed to progeny. Which feature best explains the plasmid's continued replication?
options: The plasmid possesses its own origin of replication, allowing it to replicate independently of the chromosome
The plasmid integrates into the chromosome and is copied along with it
The resistance gene itself functions as a replication enzyme
The plasmid relies entirely on the chromosomal origin for its copying
explain: Plasmids carry their own origin of replication, so their copying is initiated independently of the chromosome and can proceed even when chromosomal replication is blocked. If the plasmid depended on the chromosomal origin or had to integrate into the chromosome to be copied, halting chromosome replication would stop the plasmid too, contradicting the observation. An antibiotic-resistance gene specifies a resistance product, not a DNA-replication enzyme, so it cannot account for the plasmid's autonomous copying.
hint: Consider what a piece of DNA needs in order to start its own replication without waiting on the main chromosome.

q: The F factor of E. coli can exist as an autonomous circular DNA element but is also able to insert itself into the bacterial chromosome and be replicated as part of it. Which term best classifies a genetic element with this dual capability?
options: Episome
Auxotroph
Prototroph
Colony
explain: An element that can replicate autonomously as a plasmid yet also integrate into and replicate with the chromosome is defined as an episome, and the F factor is the classic example. A prototroph and an auxotroph are nutritional categories describing a cell's biosynthetic abilities, not types of DNA elements. A colony is a visible cluster of cells derived from a single founder, again not a description of a DNA element with integration ability.
hint: Look for the word reserved for a plasmid that can also join the chromosome.

q: While most bacteria carry their genetic information on a single circular chromosome, a few species deviate from this arrangement. Which example correctly pairs a species with its atypical genome organization?
options: Vibrio cholerae maintains two separate chromosomes
E. coli normally maintains three chromosomes
All bacteria package their genome on linear chromosomes
Rhizobium survives with no chromosome at all
explain: Vibrio cholerae is a well-documented exception to the single-chromosome rule, carrying two distinct circular chromosomes of unequal size. E. coli follows the typical pattern of one circular chromosome rather than three. While a small number of bacteria do have linear chromosomes, this is the exception, not a universal feature, so claiming every bacterium is linear is incorrect. No bacterium can survive without any chromosome at all, since the chromosome carries essential genes; Rhizobium in fact has one chromosome plus several large plasmids, so describing it as chromosome-less is wrong.
hint: Think about whether a deviation could be a difference in chromosome number, shape, or absence, then judge which pairing is actually documented and biologically possible.

q: A geneticist wants to confirm that an antibiotic-resistance gene resides on a plasmid rather than on the chromosome. Cells are grown under conditions that cause loss of the plasmid (curing) without harming the chromosome. If the gene is plasmid-borne, what outcome is expected for the cured cells?
options: They lose antibiotic resistance because the gene was carried on the now-absent plasmid
They retain resistance because curing deletes only chromosomal genes
They die immediately, since the plasmid carries genes essential for viability
They become prototrophic for the first time after losing the plasmid
explain: Plasmids typically carry nonessential but useful genes, such as antibiotic resistance, and replicate separately from the chromosome. If the resistance gene sits on a plasmid, eliminating that plasmid removes the gene and the cells become sensitive. Curing targets the plasmid, not the chromosome, so claiming it deletes chromosomal genes is backward. Because plasmid genes are generally nonessential, losing the plasmid should not be lethal, ruling out immediate death. Curing removes genetic material rather than conferring new biosynthetic abilities, so the cells would not suddenly gain prototrophy.
hint: Think about where a nonessential resistance gene must reside if eliminating one independent replicon abolishes resistance.

q: A single bacterial cell is spread so thinly on an agar plate that individual cells are far apart, and after incubation discrete round colonies appear. Why are the cells within one such colony considered genetically identical?
options: Each colony arose from one founder cell whose repeated division produced a clone
Each colony formed by the fusion of many unrelated cells at one spot
Colony cells exchange chromosomes during growth to become uniform
Each colony represents a mixture of prototrophs and auxotrophs that happened to settle together
explain: When cells are well separated, a visible colony grows up from a single deposited cell that divides repeatedly, so all its descendants form a clone with the same genotype. Colonies do not form by many unrelated cells merging at one point under proper dilution, so the fusion idea is wrong. Routine vegetative growth does not involve cells swapping whole chromosomes to homogenize themselves, so that mechanism does not explain the uniformity. A colony from one founder is not a settled mixture of different nutritional types; that scenario would require multiple distinct founders, which dilution is designed to avoid.
hint: Trace a single well-separated cell forward through many rounds of division and ask what its offspring share.

q: A researcher mixes a culture of donor cells with recipient cells and observes that genetic material moves from donors to recipients, but never from recipients back to donors during the same contact. Which feature of bacterial conjugation does this observation directly illustrate?
options: Transfer is unidirectional, with no reciprocal exchange between the two partners
Both partners simultaneously swap equal-sized fragments of DNA
Genetic material is exchanged only after both cells divide
DNA is released into the medium and taken up by whichever cell is competent
explain: Conjugation moves DNA strictly one way, from donor to recipient through a direct cytoplasmic connection; the recipient does not send any DNA back, so there is no reciprocal exchange. Simultaneous two-way swapping does not occur in conjugation. Transfer is not contingent on cell division, and DNA passing through naked medium uptake describes transformation, a different process entirely.
hint: Think about the direction DNA travels and whether the recipient ever gives anything in return during a single mating.

q: Which statement most accurately describes the genetic state of an F+ cell?
options: It carries the F factor integrated into its chromosome and transfers chromosomal genes at high frequency
It carries the F factor as an autonomous plasmid separate from the chromosome and acts as a donor
It lacks the F factor entirely and serves only as a recipient
It carries an F factor that has picked up a segment of chromosomal DNA after excision
explain: An F+ cell holds the F factor as a free-standing plasmid that is physically independent of the chromosome, and this makes it a donor. A factor integrated into the chromosome that drives high-frequency chromosomal transfer describes an Hfr cell. A cell lacking F is a recipient, not a donor. An F element carrying chromosomal genes after excision describes an F-prime.
hint: Where does the F factor physically reside in this cell, and is that location part of the chromosome or separate from it?

q: When an F+ cell mates with an F- cell, what is the typical outcome for the recipient?
options: The recipient receives a large block of chromosomal genes but remains F-
The recipient becomes F+ but does not usually acquire donor chromosomal genes
The recipient becomes an Hfr cell capable of high-frequency transfer
The recipient becomes a stable partial diploid for several chromosomal loci
explain: In this mating the autonomous F plasmid is what gets transferred, so the recipient is converted to F+, while donor chromosomal genes are generally not carried along. Receiving a large block of chromosomal genes is characteristic of an Hfr mating, not an F+ cross. The recipient does not become an Hfr from this event, since that requires integration of F into the chromosome. A stable partial diploid for chromosomal loci results from an F-prime mating, not from a standard F+ cross.
hint: Consider what physical element is free to move when F sits as a plasmid, and whether the chromosome comes along with it.

q: An Hfr strain is crossed with an F- strain and conjugation proceeds without interruption for a long time. Why does the recipient almost always remain F- despite prolonged contact?
options: The integrated F is nicked so that the chromosome enters first and the trailing portion of F rarely completes the journey
Recipient cells actively degrade any F sequences immediately upon entry
The F factor in an Hfr is permanently non-transferable once it integrates
The sex pilus retracts before any portion of the F factor can begin to move
explain: When the integrated F is nicked, the transfer process splits the F sequence so that part of it leads and the remainder trails behind the entire chromosome; because the whole chromosome rarely transfers before the bridge breaks, the trailing F portion almost never arrives intact, leaving the recipient F-. Active degradation of F is not the explanation. Integrated F is in fact transferable, which is precisely why chromosomal genes follow it. The pilus does establish contact and transfer begins, so failure of the pilus is not the cause.
hint: Where does the F sequence get split during transfer, and how much of the chromosome would have to cross for the recipient to receive a complete F?

q: In an interrupted-conjugation experiment, an Hfr x F- mating is stopped at successive time points and recipients are scored for donor markers. A student proposes plotting recombination frequency to assign map distances. What is the conceptual flaw in this proposal?
options: Recombination frequency cannot be measured in bacteria because they are haploid
Map position in this method is determined by the time a marker first enters, expressed in minutes, not by recombination frequency
Recombination frequency only applies when both parents transfer DNA reciprocally
Distances in this method must be converted to physical base pairs before any analysis
explain: Interrupted-mating mapping orders genes by the time, in minutes, at which each marker first appears in recipients, so the map metric is time of entry rather than recombination frequency. The claim that recombination frequency cannot be measured in bacteria is false; it is used in other mapping contexts. The reciprocal-transfer statement misstates how recombination frequency works. Converting to base pairs is not a required step of this technique, which yields a time-based map.
hint: What units does this mapping technique report, and what physical event is being timed as conjugation proceeds?

q: Three donor markers enter an F- recipient at 6, 14, and 27 minutes in an interrupted-conjugation experiment. What can be concluded about their arrangement relative to the origin of transfer?
options: All three lie equidistant from the origin and from one another
The marker entering at 6 minutes is closest to the origin and the one at 27 minutes is farthest
The marker entering at 27 minutes is closest to the origin because it transfers last
The entry times reveal nothing about order without recombination data
explain: Markers nearer the origin of transfer cross the bridge sooner, so the one appearing at 6 minutes lies closest to the origin and the one at 27 minutes lies farthest along the transferred segment. Equal spacing is contradicted by the unequal entry times. The idea that the latest-entering marker is closest reverses the actual relationship. Entry times alone do establish order, which is the entire premise of interrupted mapping.
hint: If transfer begins at a fixed origin and proceeds steadily, does an early arrival mean near or far from that starting point?

q: A geneticist recovers an F element that has excised from an Hfr chromosome but has carried a few adjacent chromosomal genes along with it. When this element is transferred to an F- recipient, what distinctive genetic state results?
options: A merozygote that is partially diploid for the chromosomal genes carried on the element
A fully haploid cell that has lost its original chromosomal copies of those genes
A cell that immediately becomes an Hfr with the same integration site
A recipient that remains F- because the carried genes block transfer
explain: An F-prime element brings a few chromosomal genes into the recipient, which already has its own chromosomal copies, producing a partial diploid known as a merozygote for those loci. The recipient does not lose its native copies, so a fully haploid result is incorrect. Becoming an Hfr requires integration, which is not the immediate outcome of receiving an autonomous F-prime. The carried genes do not block transfer; the element transfers readily and converts the recipient.
hint: If the incoming element carries extra copies of genes the recipient already has, how many copies of those genes now exist in one cell?

q: Hfr cells arise from F+ populations, but only at a frequency of roughly 1 in 10,000. What event accounts for the conversion of an F+ cell into an Hfr cell?
options: Loss of the F plasmid followed by uptake of chromosomal DNA from the medium
Integration of the autonomous F factor into the bacterial chromosome
Duplication of the F plasmid so that two copies circulate freely
Acquisition of a second sex pilus that doubles transfer efficiency
explain: An Hfr cell is generated when the free F plasmid integrates into the host chromosome, after which it can mobilize chromosomal genes at high frequency; this is a relatively rare event. Losing F and taking up naked DNA does not create an Hfr and conflates conjugation with transformation. Duplicating the plasmid keeps F autonomous and does not produce an Hfr. There is no mechanism by which gaining a second pilus converts the cell type.
hint: What has to happen to the location of the F factor for a donor to start transferring chromosomal genes at high frequency?

q: During the transfer of single-stranded DNA in conjugation, the strand is nicked at a specific site and one end leads into the recipient. Which description correctly captures the early molecular events of this transfer?
options: A nick is made at the origin of transfer and the 5' end of the nicked strand enters the recipient first
Both DNA strands are nicked and enter the recipient together as a duplex
The strand enters 3' end first while the donor retains the 5' end
Transfer begins only after the entire plasmid has been replicated into two duplexes
explain: Transfer starts with a nick at the origin of transfer, and the freed strand moves into the recipient leading with its 5' end, after which both cells synthesize the complementary strand. A double-stranded duplex is not what crosses; a single strand transfers. The leading end is the 5' end, not the 3' end, so that description is reversed. Replication into two complete duplexes before transfer is not how the rolling process works; synthesis occurs in concert with transfer.
hint: Recall which terminus of the nicked single strand is threaded across first, and whether one or two strands make the trip.

q: Two Hfr strains derived from the same F+ ancestor transfer the same set of chromosomal markers but in different sequential orders and starting from different first markers. What best explains these differing transfer profiles?
options: The two strains differ in the chromosomal site and orientation at which F integrated
One strain is actually an F- cell that cannot transfer chromosomal genes
The strains carry different chromosomes with unrelated gene content
Differences arise only from random errors in scoring recipient markers
explain: The order and starting point of marker transfer are set by where F integrated into the chromosome and in which orientation, so two Hfr strains with different integration sites will lead with different markers and proceed in different directions. An F- cell would not transfer chromosomal genes at all, contradicting the observed transfer. Both strains share the same chromosome, so claiming unrelated gene content is wrong. Consistent, reproducible differences in order are not explained by random scoring errors.
hint: What determines which marker leads and which direction transfer proceeds when F is part of the chromosome?

q: A microbiologist takes a culture of naturally competent bacteria during the brief window when competence is highest, adds purified linear DNA carrying a wild-type allele, and recovers a few stable transformants. Which statement best describes what happened to the donor DNA molecule as it entered and became part of the recipient genome?
options: Both strands of the donor fragment entered intact and the whole double-stranded piece was inserted between two adjacent recipient genes
One strand was degraded as the other crossed the membrane and integrated into the homologous region by two crossover events
The donor fragment circularized inside the cell and replicated independently as a small plasmid
A recipient enzyme copied the donor sequence without the donor DNA ever physically entering the cell
explain: During transformation, the entering single strand replaces the homologous recipient strand through a pair of crossover (recombination) events, while the complementary donor strand is degraded at the surface. The idea that an intact double helix is spliced in whole, that the fragment becomes an autonomously replicating plasmid, or that the sequence is copied without physical uptake all misrepresent the strand-degradation-plus-double-recombination mechanism that defines chromosomal transformation.
hint: Picture what physically happens to the two strands of the incoming DNA, and what it takes for any of it to become a lasting part of the chromosome.

q: In a competent bacterial population, which factor would be expected to INCREASE the number of cells that successfully take up and integrate exogenous DNA?
options: Lowering the concentration of available DNA in the medium
Raising the concentration of available DNA in the medium
Removing all sequence homology between donor and recipient DNA
Sampling the culture long after it has left the competent growth phase
explain: Uptake efficiency rises with the amount of DNA presented to competent cells, so a higher concentration boosts transformation. Reducing DNA concentration does the opposite, eliminating homology blocks the recombination needed for stable chromosomal integration, and waiting until cells have passed their competent growth phase lowers the fraction of cells able to take up DNA.
hint: Competence is a state that depends on growth stage; once a cell is competent, what about the surrounding DNA supply would make a hit more likely?

q: A lab strain of E. coli does not become competent on its own. A student wants to introduce a plasmid into it. Which combination of treatments is a standard way to make these cells take up the DNA?
options: Treating cells with calcium chloride and a brief heat shock, or applying an electric pulse
Starving the cells of all nutrients for several days until they lyse
Infecting the cells with a temperate phage that lacks any DNA
Exposing the cells to ultraviolet light to induce the SOS response
explain: Because this strain is not naturally competent, competence must be induced artificially, classically with calcium chloride plus heat shock or with electroporation. Prolonged starvation to the point of lysis destroys the cells rather than transforming them, an empty phage delivers no DNA, and UV-induced SOS triggers a repair response rather than membrane permeabilization for plasmid uptake.
hint: For organisms that won't take up DNA naturally, what physical or chemical tricks do molecular biologists routinely use to force the membrane to admit DNA?

q: Three genes are tested in cotransformation experiments. Genes p and q are frequently cotransformed, genes q and r are frequently cotransformed, but p and r are almost never cotransformed. What is the most likely linear order of these genes?
options: p - r - q
q - p - r
p - q - r
r - p - q
explain: Cotransformation frequency falls as the distance between genes grows, because donor DNA is fragmented and only nearby genes ride the same piece. Frequent cotransformation of the two end-pairs but near-zero for the remaining pair places the shared gene in the middle, giving the order in which the rarely cotransformed pair sits farthest apart. The other arrangements would put a frequently cotransformed pair at the extremes or the rarely cotransformed pair adjacent, contradicting the data.
hint: The pair that is almost never cotransformed must be the farthest apart; which gene therefore has to lie between the other two?

q: Two pairs of genes are mapped by cotransformation. Pair 1 cotransforms 65% of the time and pair 2 cotransforms 12% of the time. What does this difference indicate about the physical distances between the genes?
options: The genes in pair 1 are farther apart than the genes in pair 2
The genes in pair 1 are closer together than the genes in pair 2
Both pairs are exactly the same distance apart
Cotransformation frequency carries no information about distance
explain: Cotransformation frequency is inversely related to the distance separating two genes, because genes close together are more likely to remain on the same donor fragment. A higher cotransformation frequency therefore reflects a shorter distance, so the more frequently cotransformed pair must be closer. Treating high frequency as greater distance, claiming equal distances, or denying any relationship all contradict the inverse-distance principle.
hint: Recall whether genes that stay together on a fragment more often are nearer to or farther from each other.

q: A researcher finds that a sizable fraction of a bacterium's genome closely resembles sequences from distantly related species rather than from its own ancestors. Which process most directly accounts for genes appearing in a genome through acquisition from a different species rather than inheritance from a parent?
options: Random point mutation accumulating over many generations
Horizontal gene transfer between species
Vertical transmission from parent to offspring
Polyploidization of the bacterial chromosome
explain: Acquiring DNA from another species, rather than from a direct ancestor, is the hallmark of horizontal gene transfer, which moves genes laterally via conjugation, transformation, or transduction. Point mutation alters existing sequences but does not import foreign genes, vertical transmission passes genes down a lineage within a species, and bacteria do not undergo true polyploidization to gain interspecies sequence.
hint: Distinguish inheritance down a lineage from movement of DNA across species boundaries, and ask which one explains foreign-looking sequence.

q: A hospital reports that a single bacterial strain has rapidly become resistant to several unrelated antibiotics at once, and that the resistance can move between bacterial species in the ward. Which feature best explains both the multidrug resistance and its spread across species?
options: A single chromosomal point mutation that happens to block every drug
An R plasmid carrying multiple resistance genes that transfers between cells
Loss of the cell wall, making the bacteria insensitive to all antibiotics
A reversion mutation that restores a previously inactivated gene
explain: R plasmids can carry several antibiotic-resistance genes together, accounting for simultaneous resistance to multiple unrelated drugs, and they can spread through populations and even across species by transfer. A lone point mutation rarely confers resistance to several unrelated antibiotics at once and is not itself transmissible between cells, wall loss would not grant general antibiotic insensitivity, and a reversion simply restores one gene without explaining transferable multidrug resistance.
hint: What mobile genetic element can bundle several resistance genes and also hop from one cell to another?

q: After the entering single strand of donor DNA has paired with and recombined into the homologous region of the recipient chromosome, some donor single-stranded DNA that did not integrate remains in the cell. What is the typical fate of this leftover single-stranded DNA, and what is the recipient cell now called?
options: It is replicated into a second chromosome, making the cell a merozygote
It is degraded by the cell, and the recipient is now called a transformant
It is exported back out of the cell unchanged, leaving the recipient unmodified
It is packaged into phage particles, turning the recipient into a transducing lysate
explain: Donor single-stranded DNA that fails to integrate is degraded, and the cell that has stably incorporated donor sequence is termed a transformant. The leftover strand is not used to build a second chromosome, it is broken down rather than re-exported intact, and transformation does not package DNA into phage particles, which is the separate process of transduction.
hint: Once recombination is finished, the cell tidies up the unused strand; what label applies to a cell that has taken up and kept new DNA this way?

q: A researcher places two auxotrophic bacterial strains on opposite sides of a U-shaped tube divided by a fine filter that prevents the two cell populations from touching. After incubation, prototrophic colonies are recovered from one arm. What does this result most directly demonstrate?
options: Genetic transfer occurred through a filterable agent rather than direct cell-to-cell contact
The filter was defective and allowed whole cells to pass between the two arms
A spontaneous reversion mutation restored the prototrophic phenotype
Two cells fused across the filter to form a stable diploid
explain: Because the filter blocks cell-to-cell contact, any genetic exchange must be carried by something small enough to cross it, namely a bacteriophage. This rules out a contact-dependent mechanism such as conjugation. A defective filter would be ruled out by appropriate controls, and the appearance of prototrophs depending on both strains being present argues against simple reversion, which would occur in a single strain alone. Stable diploids are not formed in this system, and fusion would require the very contact the filter prevents.
hint: Think about what the physical barrier permits to pass and what it excludes, and which transfer mechanisms each of those would allow.

q: During generalized transduction, how does a fragment of host bacterial DNA come to be carried inside a phage particle?
options: The phage reverse-transcribes host mRNA into DNA before packaging
A piece of fragmented host chromosome is mistakenly packaged into a phage coat in place of phage DNA
The phage integrates host genes into its genome only at a fixed attachment site
Host DNA is actively replicated by phage enzymes to make extra phage chromosomes
explain: In generalized transduction the host chromosome is broken into pieces during the lytic cycle, and occasionally a random bacterial fragment is enclosed in a phage head instead of phage DNA because the head-filling step is not strictly specific to phage DNA. Reverse transcription of mRNA plays no role here. Transfer tied to a fixed attachment site describes specialized, not generalized, transduction. The host DNA is degraded rather than faithfully replicated into new phage chromosomes.
hint: Consider what happens to the host chromosome during a lytic infection and how selective the head-filling step really is.

q: Which feature best distinguishes specialized transduction from generalized transduction?
options: Specialized transduction transfers RNA while generalized transduction transfers DNA
Specialized transduction requires conjugation pili while generalized transduction does not
Only genes located near the prophage's integration site can be transferred in specialized transduction
Specialized transduction can move any gene in the genome with equal probability
explain: Specialized transduction arises from imprecise excision of an integrated prophage, so only bacterial genes flanking the integration site can be picked up and transferred. Both processes move DNA, not RNA, and neither requires conjugative pili. The ability to move essentially any gene with roughly equal likelihood is the hallmark of generalized transduction, not specialized transduction.
hint: Where does the prophage sit, and which genes are physically close enough to be caught when it leaves imprecisely?

q: In a cotransduction experiment, genes A and B are cotransduced at 62%, genes A and C at 14%, and genes B and C are essentially never cotransduced together. Which gene arrangement is most consistent with these data?
options: The order is B - A - C with B and C farthest apart
The order is A - B - C with A and C closest together
The order is C - B - A with C and B closest together
All three genes are equidistant from one another
explain: Cotransduction frequency falls as the distance between genes increases, so a higher frequency means a shorter distance. The highest frequency is between A and B, placing them closest, and the lowest is between B and C, placing them farthest apart, with A and C at an intermediate spacing. Putting A between B and C satisfies all three relationships simultaneously. The alternatives contradict at least one of the measured frequencies or ignore the spread of values entirely.
hint: Translate each frequency into a relative distance, then find the single linear order where the closest and farthest pairs both fit.

q: Roughly what fraction of cells receiving a transducing phage actually become stable transductants, and why is this fraction low?
options: About 1 in 10, limited mainly by how fast the phage adsorbs to cells
Around half, limited only by whether recombination machinery is present
About 1 in 100,000 to 1 in 1,000,000, because mispackaging of host DNA and successful recombination are both rare
Essentially 100%, because every infecting phage delivers a complete host genome
explain: Transduction is inherently inefficient: only a small fraction of phage particles package host DNA, and of those, only some deliver fragments that recombine stably into the recipient chromosome, giving an overall rate near one in a hundred thousand to one in a million. A rate of one in ten or one in two would vastly overstate how often these rare events coincide, and a phage carries only a small piece of host DNA rather than an entire genome, so a near-certain outcome is impossible.
hint: Two independent low-probability steps must both succeed; consider how their rarity combines.

q: Once a transducing phage injects a fragment of donor bacterial DNA into a recipient cell, how is that DNA stably inherited as part of the recipient genome?
options: By a double crossover that exchanges the donor fragment for the homologous recipient region
By a single crossover that inserts the whole linear fragment as a circular plasmid
By integration at a specific phage attachment site shared by all transduced fragments
By the fragment replicating independently in the cytoplasm without recombination
explain: A linear donor fragment becomes a permanent part of the recipient chromosome through a double crossover, which swaps the donor segment for the homologous stretch of recipient DNA. A single crossover with a linear fragment would not yield a stable circular replicon. Site-specific integration at a phage attachment site describes prophage behavior, not the fate of a transduced gene. A linear fragment lacking an origin cannot replicate on its own, so it would be diluted out or degraded without recombination.
hint: A linear piece of DNA has two ends; think about how many exchange events are needed to splice its interior into a circular chromosome and leave it intact.

q: A bacterial geneticist wants to map two genes that are about 30% of the chromosome apart. A colleague suggests using cotransduction frequency to estimate the distance. Why is this approach unsuitable for these particular genes?
options: Cotransduction works only for genes on different replicons, not the same chromosome
A transducing phage carries only about 1% of the chromosome, so genes that far apart almost never travel together
Cotransduction frequency increases with distance, so widely spaced genes would appear falsely close
Phage DNA cannot recombine with chromosomal DNA over long stretches
explain: A phage head holds only about one percent of the bacterial chromosome, so two markers separated by roughly thirty percent of the genome can essentially never be packaged into the same particle and will show a cotransduction frequency near zero, making them unmappable by this method. Cotransduction operates on genes of the same chromosome, and the frequency decreases, not increases, with distance. Recombination itself is not the limiting issue; the physical packaging limit is what makes the genes too far apart to co-transfer.
hint: Compare the genome span between these markers to the amount of DNA a single phage particle can physically hold.

q: Which set of conditions must all be satisfied for generalized transduction to successfully transfer a bacterial gene to a recipient?
options: The phage must lysogenize the recipient, integrate at a fixed site, and then excise precisely
The host chromosome must be fragmented, host DNA must be packaged into a phage coat, and the transferred DNA must recombine into the recipient genome
The donor and recipient must form a conjugation bridge, transfer a plasmid, and circularize it
The phage must inject only its own DNA, replicate it, and lyse the recipient without recombination
explain: Generalized transduction depends on three things happening in sequence: the host chromosome is broken into fragments, a fragment is accidentally packaged into a phage coat because packaging is not phage-DNA-specific, and the delivered bacterial DNA recombines into the recipient chromosome. Lysogeny with site-specific excision describes the specialized pathway. A conjugation bridge and plasmid transfer belong to conjugation, not transduction. A phage that injects only its own DNA and lyses the cell without recombination cannot produce a transductant at all.
hint: List the events in order from donor chromosome breakdown to a stable change in the recipient, and check that each is genuinely required.

q: In a restriction-modification system, how does a bacterium avoid cutting its own chromosome at the same sequences that its restriction endonuclease recognizes in foreign DNA?
options: It adds methyl groups to bases within those recognition sequences in its own DNA
It removes the recognition sequences from its own genome during replication
It produces an inhibitor that binds the endonuclease everywhere except at foreign DNA
It keeps its chromosome single-stranded so the endonuclease cannot bind
explain: The cell's own recognition sites are chemically marked by a modification enzyme that methylates bases within them; the restriction endonuclease then fails to cut the methylated host sites but still cleaves unmethylated foreign DNA. Excising the sites from the genome would be both impossible to do precisely and would corrupt the host's own genes. A diffusible inhibitor could not distinguish host from foreign DNA the way a covalent mark on the sequence itself does. Restriction enzymes act on double-stranded DNA, and a cell cannot maintain its entire chromosome single-stranded, so strandedness is not the protective mechanism.
hint: Think about a covalent chemical tag placed on the host's own copies of the target sequence, distinguishing 'self' from 'invader.'

q: Which statement best captures why CRISPR-Cas is described as an adaptive and heritable immune system, in contrast to restriction-modification?
options: It cleaves any foreign DNA regardless of prior exposure, and the cut DNA is shared with daughter cells
It stores sequences from past invaders in the genome, and that record is copied to progeny
It methylates invader DNA so that descendants recognize the same invader by its methylation pattern
It mutates the host's own genes in response to infection, and those mutations are inherited
explain: CRISPR-Cas builds a molecular memory by inserting fragments of past invaders' DNA as spacers into a genomic array; because the array is part of the chromosome, it is replicated and passed to daughter cells, making the acquired immunity heritable. Restriction-modification, by contrast, cuts foreign DNA based on fixed recognition sequences and stores no record of specific invaders. Methylation is the marking strategy of restriction-modification, not the memory mechanism of CRISPR, and methylation marks are not the basis of CRISPR targeting. The system does not work by mutating the host's own genes; it acquires new sequence content from the invader.
hint: Consider what physically gets added to the genome after an infection, and whether that addition is replicated along with the chromosome.

q: During the expression stage of a CRISPR-Cas response, the cell first transcribes the CRISPR array. What is the immediate transcript, before it is made functional?
options: A separate short transcript for each spacer, made from its own promoter
One long precursor RNA covering many repeats and spacers, later cut up
A fresh double-stranded DNA copy of the array, inserted at the invader's genome
A batch of Cas proteins that already carry their finished guide RNAs
explain: A single leader-associated promoter drives transcription of the whole array, yielding one long precursor RNA that contains many repeat-spacer units; this precursor is then cleaved within the repeats into individual mature crRNAs. Spacers do not each have their own promoter, so they are not transcribed separately. Transcription produces RNA, not a DNA copy, and nothing is reinserted at the invader. Cas proteins are translated from separate cas genes, and guide loading happens only after the precursor is processed.
hint: Consider whether each spacer carries its own promoter, or whether one promoter reads out the entire repeat-spacer stretch at once.

q: A researcher sequences a CRISPR array from a bacterial strain and finds that spacers matching a newly emerged phage are consistently located at one specific end of the array, while spacers matching ancient phages sit at the other end. What does this arrangement most directly reveal?
options: Spacers are added preferentially at one end, so the array records the order of past infections
Older spacers are more efficient and migrate toward the active end over time
The array is read in both directions, placing recent matches at both ends equally
Spacers are inserted randomly throughout the array regardless of infection date
explain: New spacers are preferentially inserted at one end of the array during adaptation, so the spatial order of spacers serves as a chronological record of the invaders a lineage has encountered, with the most recent acquisitions clustered at the insertion-favored end. Spacers do not physically migrate or reposition themselves based on efficiency. The observation of recent matches at only one end is direct evidence against bidirectional or random insertion, since both of those would scatter recent spacers rather than concentrate them at a single end.
hint: If you know which end new entries are added to, what does the position of a spacer tell you about when it was acquired?

q: What is the role of the PAM (protospacer-adjacent motif) in a CRISPR-Cas interference event?
options: It is a short sequence within the crRNA that anneals to the Cas protein
It is a sequence flanking the protospacer in foreign DNA that the effector complex requires before cleaving
It is the palindromic repeat copied between every pair of spacers in the array
It is the methylated mark the cell adds to its own DNA to block cleavage
explain: The PAM is a short motif located immediately next to the protospacer in the invader's DNA; the effector complex must recognize this motif adjacent to a matching target before it commits to cutting. Because the PAM lies in the foreign DNA but is not copied into the spacer within the array, the system can cleave the returning invader while sparing the array itself. The PAM is not part of the crRNA, nor is it the repeat sequence of the array, and it is unrelated to host methylation, which is the strategy of restriction-modification systems.
hint: Focus on a signal present beside the target in the invader but missing from the stored spacer, and how that difference protects the cell's own array.

q: A bacterium carries a spacer that perfectly matches a region of an incoming phage genome, yet the phage still infects successfully and is not cleaved. A point mutation in the phage that abolishes interference is later mapped to the sequence immediately flanking the matched region, not within the matched region itself. What is the most likely explanation?
options: The phage mutated its protospacer so it no longer base-pairs with the crRNA
The phage lost the adjacent motif the effector complex needs to authorize cutting
The phage methylated its own DNA to mimic the host chromosome
The bacterium failed to transcribe the array, so no crRNA was made
explain: Because the spacer still matches perfectly and the disabling mutation lies just outside the matched region, the lesion most plausibly destroys the adjacent motif the effector complex must recognize before cleaving; without that motif, targeting fails even though base-pairing could occur. A change within the protospacer would alter the matched region itself, which is explicitly ruled out. Host-style methylation is not how phages typically escape CRISPR, and a transcription failure would not map to a specific position in the phage genome. The location of the escape mutation pinpoints the flanking recognition signal.
hint: If the match is intact but a neighboring base change disarms the defense, which required signal sits beside the target rather than inside it?

q: Two daughter cells arise from a bacterium that recently survived a phage infection by acquiring a new spacer. Assuming faithful replication, what immune status do the daughters inherit toward that phage?
options: Neither daughter is protected, because spacers are not copied during cell division
Both daughters inherit the new spacer and are protected against that phage
Only one daughter inherits the spacer, because each spacer segregates to a single cell
Both daughters must be re-infected before they can rebuild the protective spacer
explain: The acquired spacer is integrated into the chromosomal CRISPR array, so when the chromosome is replicated both daughter cells receive a full copy of the array, including the new spacer, and both are protected. This is precisely why CRISPR immunity is considered heritable. Spacers are part of the genome and are copied during replication, so the claim that they are not inherited is incorrect. A chromosomal insert does not segregate to only one daughter the way a single plasmid copy might, and the daughters do not need re-exposure since the memory is already encoded.
hint: Where in the cell does the new spacer live, and what happens to everything at that location when the cell divides?

q: A type II restriction endonuclease recognizes the palindromic site 5'-GAATTC-3'. A circular plasmid is found to contain three copies of this exact sequence. Assuming the enzyme cuts at every unmethylated occurrence and none of the sites are methylated, how many linear fragments result from complete digestion?
options: Two fragments
Three fragments
Four fragments
Six fragments
explain: For a closed circular DNA, the number of fragments equals the number of cut sites: the first cut opens the circle into a single linear piece, and each additional cut adds exactly one more fragment, so N cuts on a circle yield N fragments. Three cut sites therefore give three fragments. The intuition that three cuts give four pieces holds only for a linear molecule, where the two free ends mean cuts produce N+1 fragments; applying that linear rule to a circle is the classic error. Counts smaller or larger than three would require a different number of cut sites than the three present.
hint: Remember that the topology matters: cutting a closed loop at N places gives a different count than cutting a straight line at N places.

q: An engineer wants to repurpose a CRISPR-Cas effector complex to cut a specific target sequence in a synthetic DNA construct. Beyond designing a crRNA complementary to the target, what additional sequence feature must the target construct contain for the complex to cleave it?
options: A methylated version of the target so the complex can verify it is foreign
A short adjacent recognition motif positioned next to the targeted sequence
A palindromic repeat identical to those separating spacers in the natural array
A second copy of the crRNA sequence encoded directly in the construct
explain: Even with a perfectly complementary crRNA, the effector complex will not cleave the target unless a short authorizing motif lies immediately adjacent to it, because the complex checks for that flanking signal before committing to a cut. Methylation is the marking used by restriction-modification systems and is not what licenses CRISPR cleavage; in fact CRISPR targeting does not require the target to be methylated. The array's repeat sequence is not needed in the target DNA, and the target does not need to encode the crRNA, since the guide is supplied by the complex.
hint: Recall the natural requirement that lets the system tell invader DNA from its own array, and that this requirement must be designed into any engineered target.

q: A microbiologist isolates a phage that, after infecting a bacterial culture, always produces clear plaques and never establishes a stable, dormant relationship with surviving host cells. No bacteria carrying this phage's DNA in a silent integrated state can be recovered. How is this phage best classified?
options: A virulent phage, because it can only complete the lytic cycle and cannot integrate as a prophage
A temperate phage, because it forms plaques on a bacterial lawn
A virulent phage, because all phages that lyse their hosts are by definition unable to inject DNA
A temperate phage, because clear plaques indicate the lysogenic pathway is active
explain: A phage that can only lyse its host and never integrates its genome to lie dormant is restricted to the lytic cycle, which is the defining property of a virulent phage. Temperate phages are defined by the ability to choose between lysis and integration as a prophage, so a strain that never integrates is not temperate. Clear plaques actually reflect complete lysis and are characteristic of purely lytic infection, not the lysogenic pathway, which tends to produce turbid plaques because some cells survive as lysogens. The claim that lytic phages cannot inject DNA is false, since DNA injection is the first step of the lytic cycle itself.
hint: Think about what single capability separates a phage that can lie dormant from one that always destroys its host.

q: A bacterial cell carries a prophage integrated in its chromosome. A second phage of the same type then attempts to infect this cell. What is the most likely outcome and the reason for it?
options: Infection proceeds normally because the prophage and incoming phage cooperate to speed lysis
The incoming phage is blocked because the lysogen expresses immunity to superinfection by the same phage
The incoming phage immediately forces the prophage out of the chromosome and both enter the lytic cycle
The cell dies instantly because two copies of the same phage genome are always lethal
explain: A cell carrying a prophage is a lysogen, and lysogens express a repressor system that grants immunity to superinfection by the same phage, so the second infecting phage of that type cannot establish a productive infection. The incoming phage does not cooperate to accelerate lysis, nor does its arrival force excision of the resident prophage, since induction is normally triggered by stimuli such as UV light or certain chemicals rather than by a second infection of the same type. Simply having a second genome present is not automatically lethal, so the claim of instant death is incorrect.
hint: Consider what protection a cell already harboring a dormant version of the phage would have against the very same phage.

q: A researcher plates a diluted phage suspension onto a confluent lawn of sensitive bacteria and, after incubation, counts the clear zones that appear. What does each individual clear zone represent?
options: A single bacterial colony that resisted infection and grew into a visible patch
A region where the agar dried out and prevented bacterial growth
The descendants of one original infecting phage that repeatedly lysed neighboring cells
A cluster of bacteria that were converted into lysogens and stopped dividing
explain: Each clear zone, or plaque, is produced when one original phage infects a cell, lyses it, and its progeny spread to lyse surrounding cells, so the plaque represents the clonal progeny of a single founding phage. This is why counting plaques can be used to estimate phage concentration. A clear zone is not a surviving bacterial colony, which would appear as growth rather than as a hole in the lawn, and it is not an artifact of dried agar. Lysogen formation would allow cells to keep growing rather than create a clear region of lysis.
hint: Trace the zone back in time: what single starting particle could give rise to an expanding patch of destroyed cells?

q: In a phage titer experiment, 0.1 mL of a suspension diluted by a factor of one million is plated and yields 240 plaques. What is the approximate concentration of infectious phage in the original undiluted suspension?
options: 2.4 × 10⁶ phage per mL
2.4 × 10⁸ phage per mL
2.4 × 10⁹ phage per mL
2.4 × 10¹¹ phage per mL
explain: The number of plaques equals the number of infectious phage plated, so 240 phage were in the 0.1 mL plated. Dividing by the plated volume gives 240 / 0.1 = 2400 phage per mL of the diluted suspension. Multiplying by the dilution factor of one million restores the original concentration: 2400 × 10⁶ = 2.4 × 10⁹ phage per mL. Forgetting to account for the 0.1 mL volume understates the result by a factor of ten, while misapplying the dilution factor shifts the exponent up or down, producing the other values.
hint: Two corrections are needed: scale up for the small volume actually plated, then scale up for how much the sample was diluted.

q: A culture of lysogenic bacteria is growing normally with the prophage replicating quietly along with the host chromosome. A technician exposes the culture to a pulse of UV light, and soon many cells lyse and release phage particles. What event was triggered?
options: Induction, in which the prophage excises from the chromosome and enters the lytic cycle
Transformation, in which the cells took up free phage DNA from the medium
Superinfection immunity, which protected the cells and caused them to lyse
Passive replication, in which the prophage copied itself faster than the host
explain: UV light is a classic inducing stimulus that inactivates the prophage repressor, prompting the prophage to excise from the host chromosome and switch into the lytic cycle, leading to cell lysis and phage release. This is induction. Transformation refers to uptake of free DNA, which is unrelated to a prophage already resident in the chromosome. Superinfection immunity blocks new infection by the same phage and would prevent, not cause, the observed lysis. Passive replication describes the quiet copying of the prophage with the host and does not produce a burst of lysis.
hint: Recall what certain physical or chemical stresses do to a dormant prophage's control over the lytic genes.

q: Two phage strains differing at two genetic markers, host range (h) and plaque morphology (r), are used to coinfect the same bacterial cells at high multiplicity. Why is coinfection essential if the goal is to map the distance between these two genes?
options: Coinfection raises the temperature inside the host, which is required to activate phage enzymes
Coinfection lets two different phage genomes occupy one cell so homologous recombination can produce recombinants
Coinfection prevents the host from degrading any phage DNA, guaranteeing higher plaque counts
Coinfection forces both phage strains into the lysogenic cycle, where mapping is more accurate
explain: Mapping by recombination requires that two genetically distinct phage genomes be present in the same host cell at the same time, so that homologous recombination can exchange segments and generate recombinant progeny carrying new combinations of the markers. This is exactly what coinfection at high multiplicity ensures. Coinfection has nothing to do with raising internal temperature or activating enzymes, nor does it specifically prevent host degradation of phage DNA. Recombination-based mapping is carried out during productive (lytic) infection, not by forcing the phages into lysogeny.
hint: What must be true about the genomes present in a single cell before any exchange of genetic segments can occur?

q: A coinfection cross between phage strains h⁻r⁺ and h⁺r⁻ yields 8,000 total plaques. Among these, 360 are h⁺r⁺ and 340 are h⁻r⁻, while the remainder match one of the two parental genotypes. What is the recombination frequency between h and r?
options: About 4.4%
About 8.75%
About 9.1%
About 17.5%
explain: The two recombinant classes here are the genotypes that differ from both parental inputs, namely h⁺r⁺ and h⁻r⁻, which together total 360 + 340 = 700 plaques. Recombination frequency equals recombinant plaques divided by total plaques, so 700 / 8000 = 0.0875, or about 8.75%. Counting only one recombinant class halves the answer, while treating the recombinants as if total were doubled or otherwise mishandling the denominator produces the other inflated or deflated values.
hint: Identify which two genotypes are the new combinations not present in the parents, then divide their sum by everything counted.

q: A geneticist crosses phages carrying three markers and obtains the following recombination frequencies between pairs: a–b = 3%, b–c = 9%, and a–c = 12%. What is the most consistent gene order along the phage chromosome?
options: b–a–c, because a and b are closest together
a–c–b, because c lies between the two outermost genes
a–b–c, because the largest distance spans the two outermost genes
c–a–b, because a and c have the largest distance
explain: The largest recombination frequency identifies the two genes farthest apart, here a and c at 12%. The third gene, b, must lie between them, and indeed the two shorter distances add up to the longest one: a–b (3%) plus b–c (9%) equals a–c (12%). This additivity confirms the order a–b–c with b in the middle. Placing c or a in the middle would require the middle gene to be one of the outermost pair, contradicting the observation that a–c is the longest interval and that the two smaller intervals sum to it.
hint: The two genes with the largest map distance flank the third; check which arrangement makes the short distances add up to the long one.

q: During the lytic cycle, after a phage injects its DNA, one of the early actions is degradation of the host's own chromosome. What is the principal advantage this degradation provides to the infecting phage?
options: It permanently converts the host into a lysogen that protects future phage generations
It frees nucleotides and shuts down host gene expression, redirecting resources toward phage replication
It strengthens the host cell wall so that progeny phages are stored safely until needed
It triggers the host to import extra DNA from neighboring cells for the phage to use
explain: Degrading the host chromosome both releases nucleotides that can be reused to build phage genomes and halts host gene expression, so the cell's machinery and raw materials are diverted toward producing phage components. This is a hallmark of efficient lytic takeover. It does not create a lysogen, since lysogeny involves integration of phage DNA rather than destruction of host DNA. Lytic phages ultimately weaken and rupture the cell wall to release progeny rather than strengthening it, and host chromosome degradation does not cause uptake of DNA from neighboring cells.
hint: Consider what a phage gains both materially and in terms of control when the host's own genome is dismantled.

q: A virologist characterizes a newly isolated virus and finds that its particle contains an RNA genome plus a protein that can synthesize DNA using that RNA as a template. Inside an infected cell, a DNA copy of the genome becomes inserted into a host chromosome before any new viral particles appear. Which class of virus best fits these observations?
options: A retrovirus
A double-stranded DNA bacteriophage
A naked single-stranded DNA virus
An RNA virus that replicates only in the cytoplasm without a DNA stage
explain: The defining features described are an RNA genome packaged with an enzyme that copies RNA into DNA, followed by insertion of that DNA copy into a host chromosome. That combination of an RNA genome, RNA-dependent DNA synthesis, and integration into host DNA is the hallmark of this group. A DNA phage carries DNA, not RNA, and needs no RNA-to-DNA step. A naked DNA virus likewise begins with DNA and does not reverse transcribe. An RNA virus that stays in the cytoplasm and never makes a DNA copy lacks both the DNA intermediate and the chromosomal insertion step, so it cannot match the description.
hint: Focus on the unusual combination: an RNA genome that gets turned into DNA and then parked inside a chromosome. Which enzyme makes that possible, and which virus type carries it?

q: The enzyme reverse transcriptase is essential to the retroviral life cycle. What reaction does it catalyze?
options: Synthesis of a DNA strand using an RNA template
Synthesis of an RNA strand using a DNA template
Joining of viral DNA into the host chromosome
Cleavage of a long polyprotein into mature viral proteins
explain: Reverse transcriptase reads an RNA template and builds a complementary DNA strand, the step that gives the enzyme its name because it reverses the usual flow from DNA to RNA. Making RNA from a DNA template is ordinary transcription performed by RNA polymerase, not this enzyme. Inserting the viral DNA into the chromosome is carried out by integrase. Cutting a polyprotein into functional pieces is the job of a viral protease. Only the production of DNA from an RNA template describes reverse transcriptase.
hint: The name itself hints at which direction of information flow is being reversed compared with normal transcription.

q: A retrovirus genome has lost a functional copy of its pol gene through mutation, but gag and env remain intact. Assuming no help from any other virus, what is the most direct consequence for a particle carrying this defective genome after it enters a new host cell?
options: It cannot reverse transcribe its RNA or integrate, so the genome fails to become a provirus
It can still integrate normally but cannot make capsid proteins
It produces a normal envelope but cannot enter cells at all
It integrates and replicates normally but cannot bud from the cell
explain: The pol gene encodes both reverse transcriptase and integrase, the two enzymes needed to copy the RNA genome into DNA and splice that DNA into the host chromosome. Losing pol therefore blocks both reverse transcription and integration, so no provirus forms. Capsid proteins come from gag, which is intact, so coat protein synthesis is unaffected. Envelope glycoproteins come from env, which is also intact, so entry machinery is still made. Budding depends on assembly of structural proteins rather than on pol, so the specific failure caused by a pol defect is the inability to establish a provirus.
hint: Ask what two activities are bundled together in this one gene, and which steps of the cycle stop if both are gone.

q: In the retroviral life cycle, the term provirus refers specifically to which form of the viral genetic material?
options: Double-stranded viral DNA integrated into a host chromosome
The single-stranded RNA genome packaged inside the virion
The mature spliced viral mRNA being translated by ribosomes
The lipid-enveloped particle that buds from the host cell
explain: A provirus is the double-stranded DNA copy of the viral genome after it has been inserted into the chromosome of the host. The packaged RNA is the genome the virion carries before infection, not the provirus. Spliced viral mRNA is a transcript made from the provirus, but the transcript itself is not the provirus. The budding enveloped particle is the assembled virion, again distinct from the integrated DNA. Only the integrated double-stranded DNA fits the definition.
hint: Think about the word 'pro-': it describes a stage that sits inside the chromosome waiting to be expressed, in DNA form.

q: Researchers note that an effective vaccine against HIV has been extraordinarily difficult to develop, in part because the virus changes its surface proteins rapidly even within a single infected person. Which intrinsic property of HIV most directly drives this rapid antigenic change?
options: Its reverse transcriptase lacks proofreading and introduces many errors during replication
Its genome is double-stranded DNA copied with high fidelity by host polymerases
It integrates as a provirus and therefore cannot mutate at all
It replicates exclusively outside cells where no enzymes can introduce changes
explain: HIV's reverse transcriptase has no proofreading capacity, so it makes frequent errors as it copies the genome, generating a swarm of variants and a high mutation rate that fuels fast evolution within one host. This is the opposite of a faithfully copied double-stranded DNA genome, which would change slowly. Integration as a provirus does not prevent mutation; mutations are introduced during reverse transcription before and after integration over successive cycles. The virus does not replicate outside cells, so the idea that no enzymes act on it is simply incorrect. The error-prone copying enzyme is the key driver.
hint: Consider which step of copying the genome is notoriously sloppy in this virus, and whether that enzyme can correct its own mistakes.

q: HIV causes AIDS, yet the immediate cause of death in an untreated patient is usually a secondary infection by another pathogen rather than HIV itself. Which feature of HIV biology best explains this pattern?
options: It depletes helper T (CD4) lymphocytes, crippling coordination of the immune response
It directly destroys red blood cells, causing fatal oxygen starvation
It replaces the host's DNA polymerase so cells can no longer divide
It blocks the liver from clearing toxins, leading to systemic poisoning
explain: HIV preferentially infects and destroys helper T cells bearing the CD4 marker, and because those cells orchestrate the broader immune response, their loss leaves the body unable to fend off other microbes, so opportunistic secondary infections become lethal. The virus does not target red blood cells, so oxygen starvation is not the mechanism. It does not substitute for the host's DNA polymerase or globally halt cell division. It is not a liver toxin-clearance disorder. The collapse of helper T cell immunity is what opens the door to fatal secondary infections.
hint: Which immune cells does this virus single out, and what happens to the rest of the defense system when that particular cell type disappears?

q: A retrovirus is found to transform infected cells into tumor cells. Sequence analysis shows it carries a gene, in addition to the usual structural and enzymatic genes, whose product drives uncontrolled cell proliferation. This extra cancer-causing gene is best described as a(n):
options: Viral oncogene
Integrase gene
Envelope glycoprotein gene
Capsid protein gene
explain: A gene carried by a retrovirus that drives unregulated cell growth and leads to tumors is a viral oncogene, as seen in viruses historically used to study cancer. An integrase gene encodes the enzyme that inserts viral DNA into the chromosome and does not by itself transform cells. An envelope glycoprotein gene specifies surface proteins used for entry, not tumor formation. A capsid protein gene encodes coat structure. Only the oncogene accounts for the cancerous transformation of the host cells.
hint: What do we call a gene whose normal counterpart controls growth but whose viral version pushes cells toward cancer?

q: Phylogenetic studies indicate that the two main types of human immunodeficiency virus arose from separate cross-species transmission events from different nonhuman primates. Which pairing of human virus with its most likely primate source is correct?
options: HIV-1 from a chimpanzee SIV and HIV-2 from a sooty mangabey SIV
HIV-1 from a sooty mangabey SIV and HIV-2 from a chimpanzee SIV
Both HIV-1 and HIV-2 from the same sooty mangabey SIV
Both HIV-1 and HIV-2 from a single chimpanzee SIV
explain: Evidence indicates HIV-1 descended from the SIV of chimpanzees, while HIV-2 arose independently from the SIV of sooty mangabeys, two distinct primate reservoirs and two separate spillover events. Swapping the two sources reverses the established lineage. Attributing both human viruses to a single mangabey source or a single chimpanzee source ignores the evidence that the two HIV types came from different animals. The correct matching keeps the chimpanzee origin with one human type and the mangabey origin with the other.
hint: Two separate human virus types, two separate primate hosts: try to recall which great ape and which monkey each one traces back to.

q: When a newly assembled retrovirus leaves an infected animal cell, it acquires a lipid bilayer studded with viral glycoproteins as it pushes outward through the cell's surface. Considering this budding process, which statement about the viral envelope is accurate?
options: The lipid of the envelope is derived from the host cell's plasma membrane
The lipid of the envelope is synthesized entirely from scratch by viral enzymes
The envelope is made of the same protein subunits as the inner capsid
The envelope forms only after the virus has already left the cell and entered the bloodstream
explain: Enveloped viruses that bud take their lipid membrane from the host cell, so the envelope lipid originates in the host's plasma membrane even though viral glycoproteins are embedded in it. The virus does not manufacture the lipid bilayer on its own from scratch. The envelope is a lipid membrane with glycoproteins, distinct in composition from the protein capsid that surrounds the genome. Budding through the membrane is precisely how the envelope is acquired during exit, not something that happens later in the bloodstream. The host-derived lipid origin is the accurate point.
hint: As the particle squeezes out through the cell boundary, where does the surrounding fatty membrane layer come from?

q: A virologist classifies influenza as an RNA virus but insists it should NOT be grouped with retroviruses such as HIV. What feature of the influenza life cycle best justifies keeping it separate from the retroviruses?
