options: Its genetic material is never reverse-transcribed into DNA or inserted into the host chromosome
It carries its genome as double-stranded DNA rather than RNA
It replicates exclusively in the nucleus using host DNA polymerase
It lacks any lipid envelope surrounding its capsid
explain: What defines a retrovirus is the use of reverse transcriptase to make a DNA copy of its RNA genome and integrate that copy into host DNA. Influenza skips this entirely, replicating its RNA without ever passing through a DNA intermediate or integrating, which is the cleanest reason to keep it out of the retrovirus group. It does not carry DNA at all, so describing its genome as double-stranded DNA is wrong. It does not depend on host DNA polymerase for genome replication, and it is in fact an enveloped virus, so the claim about lacking an envelope is also false.
hint: Think about the one enzymatic step that earns a virus the 'retro' label, and ask whether influenza ever performs it.

q: Influenza subtype names such as H3N2 are derived from two molecules on the virus surface. What do the 'H' and 'N' designations refer to?
options: Two surface glycoproteins, hemagglutinin and neuraminidase
The number of helical and nucleocapsid layers in the capsid
Two internal polymerase subunits that copy the genome
Host-range markers for human versus avian cells
explain: Subtypes are numbered by their versions of two proteins studded on the viral envelope: hemagglutinin (H) and neuraminidase (N). These are the antigens the immune system recognizes, which is why a new H or N combination matters. The labels do not count capsid layers, they are not the polymerase subunits that replicate the genome internally, and they are not host-range tags, even though the proteins do influence which cells the virus binds.
hint: The two letters stand for proteins sitting on the outside of the virus that your immune system can see.

q: Why does antigenic shift, rather than antigenic drift, tend to be the event that triggers a global influenza pandemic?
options: It abruptly creates a virus so novel that most people have little or no pre-existing immunity to it
It permanently inactivates the host's ability to make any antibodies
It always makes the virus resistant to every antiviral drug at once
It converts the virus into a retrovirus that hides in the genome
explain: Shift swaps in whole new surface-protein segments, so the resulting virus can display antigens the population has essentially never encountered, leaving widespread susceptibility and allowing rapid global spread. Drift's incremental changes still resemble prior strains, so partial immunity blunts it. Shift does not destroy the immune system's capacity to make antibodies, it is not inherently about drug resistance, and it does not turn influenza into a genome-integrating retrovirus.
hint: Consider what happens to herd immunity when a surface protein the population has never seen suddenly appears.

q: Avian influenza subtypes rarely infect humans directly, yet pigs are often called 'mixing vessels' in flu epidemiology. What property of pigs underlies this label?
options: Their cells can be infected by human, avian, and swine influenza viruses, allowing co-infection and reassortment
They are the only animal whose cells lack the receptors avian flu needs
They convert influenza RNA into DNA so the virus can integrate
They produce antibodies that force the virus to drift faster
explain: Pigs are susceptible to influenza viruses of human, avian, and swine origin, so a single pig co-infected with viruses from different sources is an ideal venue for genome segments to reassort into a novel human-transmissible strain, as happened with the 2009 H1N1 reassortant. Pigs are not defined by lacking avian receptors; the point is the opposite, that they can host multiple sources. They do not reverse-transcribe flu RNA, and their antibody response is not what accelerates drift.
hint: Ask which animal can simultaneously catch flu from birds, humans, and its own species, setting the stage for segment swapping.

q: SARS-CoV-2 differs structurally from influenza in how its genome is organized. Which description correctly characterizes the SARS-CoV-2 genome?
options: A single continuous strand of RNA roughly 30,000 nucleotides long
Seven to eight separate RNA segments packaged together
A circular double-stranded DNA chromosome
A single RNA molecule that is reverse-transcribed before replication
explain: SARS-CoV-2 carries one continuous single-stranded RNA molecule of about 30,000 nucleotides, in contrast to flu's segmented genome. Because it is not segmented, it cannot undergo the segment reassortment that drives influenza shift. It is RNA, not a DNA chromosome, and as a coronavirus it replicates its RNA directly without the reverse-transcription step that defines retroviruses.
hint: Think about what limits influenza's reassortment ability and contrast that with the coronavirus's nucleic acid type and replication strategy.

q: Public health investigators sequenced SARS-CoV-2 samples from a cluster of patients and used the small number of accumulated mutations distinguishing each sample to reconstruct who likely infected whom. What principle makes this kind of contact tracing possible?
options: As the RNA genome replicates it gradually acquires mutations, so related infections share a traceable mutational signature
The virus integrates into each patient's DNA, leaving a permanent host barcode
Each patient's immune system rewrites the viral genome in a unique pattern
Reassortment of genome segments uniquely tags every transmission event
explain: Each round of replication introduces a few mutations, so viruses linked by recent transmission carry nearly identical genomes that diverge predictably over time, letting investigators infer transmission chains from shared and distinguishing mutations. The coronavirus does not integrate into host DNA, the host immune system does not rewrite the viral sequence, and SARS-CoV-2 has a single unsegmented genome so reassortment of segments is not the mechanism at work.
hint: Think about how steadily accumulating copying errors can act like a molecular clock linking related infections.

q: A molecule is proposed as the hereditary material of a newly discovered organism. Which property is NOT among the fundamental requirements that any genetic material must satisfy?
options: It must be able to store complex instructions for traits
It must be copied accurately when cells divide
It must be expressible into a phenotype
It must remain chemically identical across all individuals of a species
explain: The core requirements are storing complex information, replicating faithfully, encoding the phenotype through expression, and having the capacity to vary. Remaining identical across all individuals contradicts the variation requirement; genetic material must be able to differ so that species and individuals can differ. The other three describe genuine, essential demands placed on any hereditary molecule.
hint: Three of these are real demands on hereditary material; one describes the opposite of something genetic material absolutely must be able to do.

q: For much of the early twentieth century many biologists assumed proteins, not DNA, carried hereditary information. What feature of proteins most directly drove this assumption?
options: Proteins are built from twenty different amino acids, giving great combinatorial diversity
Proteins are physically larger than nucleic acids in the cell
Proteins are found only in the nucleus alongside chromosomes
Proteins replicate themselves more accurately than nucleic acids
explain: With twenty distinct amino acid building blocks, proteins seemed capable of encoding the enormous diversity of instructions that heredity requires, which is why they were favored. Size alone does not confer information capacity, proteins are not restricted to the nucleus, and proteins do not self-replicate, so those reasons did not motivate the preference.
hint: Think about what makes a molecule seem capable of holding lots of different messages rather than where it sits or how big it is.

q: Levene's tetranucleotide hypothesis slowed acceptance of DNA as the genetic material. Why did this hypothesis make DNA seem an unlikely information carrier?
options: It claimed DNA could not be copied during cell division
It proposed DNA was a monotonous repeat of a fixed four-nucleotide unit
It argued DNA was confined to the cytoplasm rather than the nucleus
It stated DNA lacked the chemical stability to persist over time
explain: The tetranucleotide hypothesis pictured DNA as a uniform, repeating block of the four nucleotides in fixed proportion. Such regularity would leave no room for the varied sequences needed to encode complex instructions, making DNA look too simple. The hypothesis was not about replication, cellular location, or stability, so those framings misstate it.
hint: Recall what made DNA seem too 'boring' to hold a complex message, given its limited set of building blocks.

q: A researcher compares two nucleic acids and notes that one is markedly more resistant to spontaneous hydrolysis than the other. Which statement best explains why this stability is advantageous for the long-term storehouse of genetic information?
options: Greater reactivity in DNA lets it mutate faster and store more variation
DNA's greater chemical stability helps it preserve information faithfully over long periods
RNA's reactivity makes it the preferred molecule for permanent storage
Stability prevents a molecule from ever being copied or expressed
explain: Because DNA is chemically more stable and less reactive than RNA, it can hold the same sequence intact across many cell generations, which suits a long-term archive. High reactivity would degrade stored information rather than improve it, RNA's reactivity makes it less suited to permanent storage, and stability does not block copying or expression.
hint: For something meant to last across generations, ask whether you want a molecule that holds together or one that readily breaks down.

q: A geneticist studies a population in which a single gene exists in several sequence variants, each producing a slightly different enzyme. Which fundamental requirement of genetic material does this observation most directly illustrate?
options: The capacity to vary
Faithful replication
Chemical stability over time
Confinement to the nucleus
explain: Multiple sequence variants of one gene within a population are a direct expression of the requirement that genetic material be able to vary, accounting for differences among individuals. Faithful replication concerns copying accuracy, stability concerns persistence, and nuclear location is not one of the fundamental requirements, so none of those captures the existence of allelic differences.
hint: Different versions of the same gene across individuals points to one specific thing genetic material must be capable of.

q: An engineered polymer perfectly stores instructions, copies itself with high fidelity, and can mutate, yet the cell possesses no machinery to read it into RNA or protein. Which requirement of genetic material is unmet, and what is the consequence?
options: Replication fails, so daughter cells receive no copies
Expression fails, so the stored information cannot shape the phenotype
Variation fails, so all offspring are genetically identical
Storage fails, so no instructions exist to begin with
explain: Stored information only matters if it can be transcribed and translated to build the organism's traits; without read-out machinery the requirement to encode the phenotype is unmet, so the instructions cannot influence form or function. The scenario explicitly grants successful storage, replication, and the ability to mutate, so those requirements are not the ones violated.
hint: Information that can be stored and copied still does nothing for the organism unless it can be turned into something. What step is missing?

q: Two genome archives store the same total amount of data. Archive A uses an alphabet of two symbols arranged in a strictly repeating pattern; Archive B uses the same two symbols but in an arbitrary, non-repeating order. Why can Archive B carry far more genetic information than Archive A despite using identical symbols?
options: A larger symbol alphabet always determines information capacity regardless of order
Information capacity comes from the freedom to arrange symbols in many distinct sequences, which a rigid repeat lacks
Repeating patterns store more information because they are easier to copy
The number of symbols alone fixes capacity, so the two archives must be equal
explain: Information depends on how many distinct messages a sequence can represent, and freely ordered symbols generate astronomically more possible sequences than a forced repeat, which encodes essentially one pattern. This is exactly why a non-repeating arrangement of just four nucleotides can carry vast information while a monotonous repeat cannot. The size of the alphabet does not by itself fix capacity when arrangement is constrained, and ease of copying is unrelated to how much a sequence can encode.
hint: Compare how many different messages you could write with the same letters when their order is free versus locked into a fixed repeat.

q: In Griffith's experiments, mice injected with a mixture of living rough (R) pneumococci and heat-killed smooth (S) pneumococci died, and living S bacteria were recovered from their blood. What does the recovery of living S cells most directly demonstrate?
options: Heat treatment only temporarily inactivated the S cells, which later revived inside the mouse
Something from the dead S cells permanently altered the R cells so their descendants were also S
The mouse immune system converted harmless R cells into virulent S cells
The R and S strains physically fused to create a new hybrid bacterium
explain: Living S cells appeared and bred true as S, meaning the trait was inherited by the descendants of the R cells. This is the hallmark of a heritable change: a substance from the dead cells altered the genetic makeup of living recipients. Heat-killing was confirmed lethal because heat-killed S alone did not kill mice, so revival is ruled out. The host's immune system does not create virulence factors. Fusion of two bacterial cells into a hybrid was not occurring and would not explain a stable inherited change in a clonal lineage.
hint: Focus on the fact that the recovered S cells passed the S trait to their offspring. What kind of change is inherited?

q: Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty treated samples of the purified transforming substance with several enzymes before testing whether each sample could still transform R cells into S cells. Treatment with which enzyme would be expected to ABOLISH transforming activity?
options: A protease that degrades polypeptides
A ribonuclease that degrades RNA
A deoxyribonuclease that degrades DNA
A lipase that degrades membrane lipids
explain: If the transforming principle is DNA, then destroying DNA should destroy transforming activity, so the DNA-degrading enzyme abolishes transformation. Degrading protein or RNA left activity intact in their experiments, arguing those molecules are not the hereditary material. A lipid-degrading enzyme would not affect a nucleic-acid-based principle either. Only the enzyme that targets DNA eliminates the effect.
hint: Which molecule must be intact for transformation to work, according to their conclusion?

q: Hershey and Chase grew separate batches of T2 bacteriophage, one labeled with 35S and the other with 32P, then allowed each batch to infect bacteria, agitated the cultures in a blender, and centrifuged them. Why were these two specific radioisotopes chosen to tag different phage components?
options: Sulfur concentrates in DNA while phosphorus concentrates in protein coats
Sulfur is found in protein but essentially absent from DNA, while phosphorus is abundant in DNA but absent from these proteins
Both isotopes label DNA, allowing the experimenters to double-check each result
Sulfur and phosphorus both label protein, distinguishing coat from tail proteins
explain: The design works because each isotope marks one molecule class exclusively: sulfur occurs in certain amino acids but not in nucleic acids, and phosphorus occurs in the phosphate backbone of nucleic acids but not in these proteins. This lets the experimenters follow protein and DNA separately. Reversing the assignments is chemically backward. Having both isotopes label the same molecule, or both label protein, would make it impossible to tell DNA and protein apart, defeating the purpose of the experiment.
hint: Recall which element is unique to protein's side chains and which is unique to the nucleic acid backbone.

q: After the blender step in the Hershey-Chase experiment, the infected bacteria were spun into a pellet while the sheared-off phage coats stayed in the supernatant. Which result best supports the conclusion that DNA is the genetic material?
options: Most of the 35S stayed in the supernatant while most of the 32P was found in the pellet with the bacteria
Most of the 32P stayed in the supernatant while most of the 35S was found in the pellet
Both isotopes were found equally distributed between pellet and supernatant
Both isotopes were found almost entirely in the supernatant
explain: Genetic material must enter the host to direct production of progeny phage. The DNA-marking label being inside the bacterial pellet, while the protein-marking label washed off into the liquid, shows that DNA entered the cell and protein stayed outside. The reversed pattern would point to protein as the injected material. An even split, or both labels staying outside, would fail to distinguish which molecule actually entered, so neither could support the DNA conclusion.
hint: The hereditary molecule must end up inside the cell that produces new phage. Which label tracks that molecule?

q: A student analyzes DNA from a newly described organism and reports the following base percentages: adenine 28%, thymine 28%, guanine 22%, cytosine 22%. A second sample, claimed to be double-stranded DNA from a different species, reads adenine 31%, thymine 19%, guanine 25%, cytosine 25%. What is the most reasonable interpretation?
options: Both samples are consistent with normal double-stranded DNA
The first sample violates Chargaff's rules but the second is consistent with them
The second sample is anomalous because its base ratios do not fit those expected for double-stranded DNA
Neither sample can be DNA because the four bases are not present in equal amounts
explain: In double-stranded DNA, the amount of adenine equals thymine and guanine equals cytosine because of complementary pairing. The first sample obeys this (28=28, 22=22). The second sample has adenine far exceeding thymine (31 vs 19), which is inconsistent with standard base pairing, flagging it as anomalous. The idea that all four bases must be equal reflects the discredited tetranucleotide hypothesis, not Chargaff's actual findings, so requiring 25% of each is wrong.
hint: Compare adenine to thymine and guanine to cytosine in each sample. Which pairing should hold?

q: Before Chargaff's analyses, the tetranucleotide hypothesis held that DNA was a monotonous, repeating unit of the four bases in equal proportions. Which feature of Chargaff's data most directly undermined this hypothesis and helped make DNA a credible candidate for the genetic material?
options: The finding that adenine always pairs with thymine inside the double helix
The finding that overall base composition differs from one species to another
The finding that the sugar-phosphate backbone is identical in all organisms
The finding that DNA absorbs ultraviolet light at 260 nm
explain: A genetic material must carry information that varies between organisms. Chargaff showed that the proportions of bases differ among species, so DNA is not a uniform, information-poor molecule but one varied enough to encode different hereditary instructions. A fixed equal-ratio repeat would carry no species-specific information. Pairing rules within the helix were established later by structural work, not by the composition data that refuted the repeating-unit idea. A uniform backbone and UV absorbance are shared chemical features that say nothing about information capacity.
hint: Think about what a hereditary molecule needs in order to specify different traits in different species.

q: Watson and Crick's 1953 model of DNA structure relied heavily on experimental data they did not generate themselves. Which contribution was most essential to deducing that DNA is a helix of specific dimensions?
options: X-ray diffraction images produced by Rosalind Franklin and Maurice Wilkins
Griffith's mouse infection experiments with pneumococci
Hershey and Chase's radioactive phage labeling results
Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty's enzyme-treatment experiments
explain: Determining the three-dimensional shape and dimensions of the molecule required diffraction data, and the X-ray images from Franklin and Wilkins revealed the helical, regular structure that the model had to fit. The other studies identified DNA as the hereditary material but provided no information about its physical geometry, so they could not have specified a helix of particular dimensions. Only the diffraction work addressed structure.
hint: Which technique reveals the physical shape and spacing of a molecule rather than its biological function?

q: Suppose a researcher repeats the Hershey-Chase experiment but uses a phage in which both the coat and the genetic material are made of protein (a hypothetical protein-only phage). Assuming the experiment is otherwise unchanged, what result would you predict?
options: The 32P label would now be found inside the bacterial pellet
Radioactive sulfur, not phosphorus, would track the material that enters the bacteria and appears in progeny
No radioactive label would enter the bacteria at all
Both sulfur and phosphorus would enter the bacteria in equal amounts
explain: If the hereditary molecule were protein, then the protein-specific marker would follow the material that enters the host and shows up in offspring, reversing the real experiment's outcome. Because this hypothetical particle contains no nucleic acid, there is no phosphate backbone to carry the phosphorus label, so phosphorus could not enter or even be incorporated. The genetic material must enter the cell, so claiming no label enters is wrong, and equal entry of both markers would not identify which molecule is hereditary.
hint: If protein carried the genes, which element would mark the molecule that goes inside and ends up in new phage?

q: A central reason DNA was initially doubted as the genetic material, while protein was favored, was an assumption about each molecule's capacity to store information. Which statement best captures the reasoning that originally favored protein?
options: Proteins are built from twenty different amino acids, suggesting far more combinations than a four-letter nucleic acid
Proteins are located in the nucleus whereas DNA is confined to the cytoplasm
Proteins can be copied during cell division but DNA cannot be replicated
Proteins are chemically more stable than DNA and resist all enzymatic degradation
explain: The historical bias toward protein rested on diversity of subunits: with twenty amino acids, proteins seemed capable of vastly more combinations than a molecule of only four bases, which was wrongly assumed too simple to encode complex heredity. The other claims are factually false: DNA resides in the nucleus, DNA is the molecule that is faithfully replicated, and DNA is not uniquely indestructible since it is readily cut by DNA-degrading enzymes. Only the argument about subunit variety reflects the actual reasoning of the time.
hint: Consider how many distinct building blocks each molecule has and how that was thought to relate to information capacity.

q: A biochemist isolates a single nucleotide and notes that the sugar carries a hydroxyl group at its 2' carbon. What can be concluded about this molecule?
options: It belongs to RNA and is therefore more chemically reactive than its DNA counterpart
It belongs to DNA, where the missing oxygen increases backbone stability
It must contain thymine rather than uracil because of the extra oxygen
It cannot form phosphodiester bonds due to the additional hydroxyl
explain: A hydroxyl at the 2' position marks the sugar as ribose, identifying the molecule as part of RNA; that reactive 2'-OH makes RNA more prone to hydrolysis and thus less stable than DNA. DNA's sugar has only hydrogen at 2', so a 2'-OH rules out DNA. The base present (thymine vs uracil) is independent of the 2' position. The 2'-OH does not block phosphodiester linkage, which uses the 3' and 5' carbons.
hint: Think about which sugar carries an oxygen at the 2' carbon and what that reactive group does to molecular stability.

q: In a polynucleotide strand, the covalent bond that connects one nucleotide to the next joins which two positions?
options: The nitrogenous base of one nucleotide to the base of the next
The 1' carbon of one sugar to the phosphate of the next
The 3'-hydroxyl of one sugar to the 5'-phosphate of the next
Two phosphate groups directly to each other across the backbone
explain: The phosphodiester bond bridges the 3'-OH of one sugar and the 5'-phosphate of the adjacent nucleotide, building the sugar-phosphate backbone. Bases pair across strands but are not the covalent link along a strand. The 1' carbon bonds to the base, not to the next phosphate. Phosphates are not joined directly to one another in the backbone.
hint: Trace the backbone: which carbon end of one sugar reaches toward the phosphate end of the neighbor?

q: A double-stranded DNA sample is found to be 32% adenine. Assuming standard Watson-Crick pairing, what percentage of the sample is guanine?
explain: By Chargaff's rules, %A equals %T, so T is also 32%, leaving 36% for G plus C combined. Because G equals C, each is half of 36%, giving 18% guanine. A value of 32% would wrongly equate G with A; 36% is the G+C total, not G alone; 68% is the remainder after A, ignoring that it must split between two bases.
hint: Pair each base with its complement first, then remember the two remaining bases share the leftover percentage equally.

q: Why does a DNA region rich in guanine-cytosine content require a higher temperature to separate its two strands than an adenine-thymine-rich region?
options: G-C pairs are held by three hydrogen bonds while A-T pairs have two
Purine-purine stacking is stronger only between G and C bases
G-C pairs share four hydrogen bonds compared to two in A-T
Cytosine and guanine are joined by covalent rather than hydrogen bonds
explain: Each G-C pair forms three hydrogen bonds versus two for each A-T pair, so G-C-rich DNA needs more thermal energy to denature. The pairing is between a purine and a pyrimidine, not purine-purine. G-C pairs have three, not four, hydrogen bonds. The bonds between paired bases are hydrogen bonds, not covalent.
hint: Count the hydrogen bonds in each base pair and consider which pairing holds the strands together more tightly.

q: One strand of a DNA duplex reads 5'-TGACCAG-3'. Reading in the conventional 5'-to-3' direction, what is the sequence of the complementary strand?
options: 5'-ACTGGTC-3'
5'-CTGGTCA-3'
5'-GACCAGT-3'
5'-TGACCAG-3'
explain: Complementary strands are antiparallel, so the partner runs 3'-to-5' opposite the given strand; pairing each base gives 3'-ACTGGTC-5', which written 5'-to-3' is CTGGTCA. Simply complementing without reversing gives the 3'-to-5' order, which is wrong when reported 5'-to-3'. Reversing without complementing or copying the original strand ignores base pairing entirely.
hint: Complement each base, but remember the partner strand points the opposite direction, so the order must be flipped.

q: A structural study reports a nucleic acid helix that is left-handed with a zigzag sugar-phosphate backbone. This form is most associated with which sequence feature?
options: A long run of consecutive adenines on one strand
Alternating purine-pyrimidine sequence such as repeated C and G
A region containing only ribonucleotides
A stretch lacking any guanine or cytosine
explain: The described left-handed, zigzag-backbone structure is Z-DNA, which is favored by alternating purine-pyrimidine sequences, classically repeating C-G. A homopolymer run of adenine does not produce the alternating pattern Z-DNA requires. Ribonucleotides describe RNA, not the Z-DNA conformation of DNA. A region without G or C cannot form the C-G alternation that promotes this form.
hint: This unusual handedness needs a repeating alternation of the two ring types; which base pair classically supplies that pattern?

q: In the standard B-form double helix, where are the nitrogenous bases located relative to the sugar-phosphate backbones?
options: Stacked toward the interior, paired between the two backbones
Projecting outward, exposed on the helix surface
Embedded within each backbone, replacing phosphate groups
Distributed randomly with no fixed position in the helix
explain: In B-DNA the hydrophilic sugar-phosphate backbones lie on the outside while the bases stack toward the helix interior, paired across the central axis. The bases are not on the exposed surface; that role belongs to the backbone. Bases do not substitute for phosphates within the backbone. Their arrangement is highly ordered, not random.
hint: Consider which parts are water-facing and which are shielded in the core when two strands wind together.

q: A virologist studies a retrovirus that copies its RNA genome into DNA inside a host cell. Which information-flow step does this represent, and how does it relate to the central dogma?
options: Transcription, a routine DNA-to-RNA step of the central dogma
Reverse transcription, an exception to the usual flow of the central dogma
Translation, the RNA-to-protein step of the central dogma
Replication, the normal duplication of a DNA template
explain: Copying RNA into DNA is reverse transcription, which runs opposite to the typical DNA-to-RNA direction and is considered an exception to the central dogma. Transcription proceeds from DNA to RNA, the reverse of what occurs here. Translation produces protein from RNA, not DNA. Replication copies DNA into DNA, not RNA into DNA.
hint: The template is RNA and the product is DNA; ask which direction the standard dogma normally forbids.

q: A molecule consists of deoxyribose joined to adenine but carries no phosphate group. How is this molecule correctly classified?
options: A nucleotide, since it contains a purine base
A nucleoside, because it is a sugar bonded to a base
A phosphodiester, owing to the deoxyribose
A pyrimidine, given that adenine is attached
explain: A sugar linked to a base without a phosphate is a nucleoside; only when a phosphate is added does it become a nucleotide. The absence of phosphate rules out the nucleotide classification. A phosphodiester is a backbone linkage, not a single sugar-base unit. Adenine is a purine, not a pyrimidine, so that label is also incorrect.
hint: Decide what adding a phosphate group changes in the name of a sugar-plus-base unit.

q: In a stretch of B-DNA measured to be roughly 34 nm long, approximately how many complete turns of the helix does it contain?
options: About 100 turns
About 34 turns
About 10 turns
About 340 turns
explain: B-DNA rises about 0.34 nm per base pair with roughly 10 bp per turn, so one full turn spans about 3.4 nm; dividing 34 nm by 3.4 nm yields about 10 turns. Estimating 100 turns confuses the per-base-pair count with turns. Reporting 34 turns mistakenly uses 1 nm per turn. A value of 340 turns inflates the result by treating each base pair as a separate turn.
hint: First find the length of a single complete turn from the rise per base pair and the base pairs per turn, then divide.

q: A circular plasmid is isolated and found to be underwound relative to its relaxed B-form state. Which statement best describes this molecule?
options: It is negatively supercoiled, which favors strand separation
It is positively supercoiled, which favors strand separation
It is negatively supercoiled, which resists strand separation
It cannot be supercoiled because supercoiling requires linear DNA
explain: Underwinding produces negative supercoiling. Because the duplex is already deficient in helical turns, the strands separate more readily, which assists processes like replication and transcription. Overwinding would be the positive state, not the underwound one. Negative supercoiling eases, rather than resists, strand opening. The claim that supercoiling needs linear DNA is backwards, since free linear ends would simply rotate and relieve any strain.
hint: Picture a helix with too few twists for its length, then ask whether that makes the two strands easier or harder to pull apart.

q: Why can a short, free linear DNA fragment with no bound proteins not maintain a stable supercoiled state?
options: Linear DNA lacks the histones needed to anchor twists
Its free ends rotate and release any torsional strain
Linear DNA is always in the A-form rather than B-form
Topoisomerases only recognize circular substrates
explain: Supercoiling stores torsional stress, and that stress can only persist if the ends are constrained. A free linear end is able to swivel, releasing the strain and returning the molecule to its relaxed state. Histones are not required to hold twists, since bacterial circular DNA supercoils without them. The form of the helix is not what permits supercoiling, and topoisomerases act on linear DNA as well, so substrate shape is not the limiting factor here.
hint: Think about what a loose end of a twisted rubber band does the moment you let go of it.

q: A researcher compares two enzymes that relieve supercoiling. Enzyme X transiently cuts a single strand of the duplex, while Enzyme Y transiently cuts both strands and passes another segment of the duplex through the break. How are these enzymes classified?
options: X is a type II topoisomerase; Y is a type I topoisomerase
Both are type I topoisomerases
X is a type I topoisomerase; Y is a type II topoisomerase
Both are helicases rather than topoisomerases
explain: A type I topoisomerase makes a transient break in one strand, whereas a type II enzyme cleaves both strands and passes a duplex segment through the gap before resealing. The enzyme that nicks a single strand fits the type I description, and the double-strand cutter fits type II, so swapping those labels is incorrect. Calling both the same type ignores the difference in how many strands are cut. Helicases unwind strands using ATP but do not break and rejoin the backbone to change linking number.
hint: Count how many strands each enzyme severs, and recall which numeral goes with cutting one versus two.

q: Histone proteins bind tightly to DNA largely because of an electrostatic attraction. Which property of histones underlies this interaction?
options: They are large and hydrophobic, excluding water from the DNA groove
They are rich in negatively charged glutamate and aspartate
They are rich in positively charged arginine and lysine residues
They carry covalently attached phosphate groups that bind DNA
explain: Histones are small basic proteins with many arginine and lysine residues, giving them a net positive charge that attracts the negatively charged phosphate backbone of DNA. Acidic residues like glutamate and aspartate would repel DNA, not bind it. Histones are not large hydrophobic proteins; their compact, charged nature is the key feature. Phosphate groups are themselves negative, so intrinsic phosphorylation would oppose DNA binding rather than drive it.
hint: DNA's backbone is negative, so consider which amino acid side chains would be drawn to it.

q: A nucleosome core particle is reconstituted in vitro. Which composition correctly describes the core?
options: About 147 bp of DNA wrapped around an octamer containing two each of H2A, H2B, H3, and H4
About 167 bp of DNA wrapped around an octamer that includes two copies of H1
About 147 bp of DNA wrapped around a tetramer of H2A, H2B, H3, and H4
About 200 bp of DNA wrapped around a hexamer lacking H3 and H4
explain: The nucleosome core is roughly 147 bp of DNA wrapped about twice around a histone octamer made of two copies each of H2A, H2B, H3, and H4. H1 is a linker histone that sits outside the core and clamps the DNA where it enters and exits, raising the protected length to about 167 bp; it is not part of the octamer. A four-protein particle is only half an octamer and would not be the canonical core. A particle missing H3 and H4 omits the central tetramer essential to the structure.
hint: Recall how many distinct core histones there are and how many copies of each assemble the spool.

q: Treating cultured cells with an inhibitor of histone deacetylases causes increased acetylation of histone tails. What is the most likely immediate consequence for the affected chromatin?
options: Tighter compaction because acetyl groups add positive charge
Looser, more open chromatin because lysine's positive charge is neutralized
No change, since acetylation alters only the DNA sequence
Permanent heterochromatin formation that cannot be reversed
explain: Acetylation adds an acetyl group to lysine side chains, neutralizing their positive charge and weakening the grip between histones and the negatively charged DNA backbone. This loosens chromatin and tends to promote transcription. Acetylation removes charge rather than adding positive charge, so it does not tighten packing. It is a covalent modification of the protein, not a change to the DNA base sequence. Because deacetylases can remove the marks, the effect is reversible, not permanent.
hint: Acetylation masks the positive charge on lysine; think about what that does to the attraction holding DNA on the histones.

q: In a karyotype, a chromosome region near the centromere stains densely, replicates late in S phase, and contains very few expressed genes. This region is best described as which of the following?
options: Euchromatin that is actively transcribed
Constitutive heterochromatin
Facultative heterochromatin that varies between cell types
Unpackaged naked DNA awaiting nucleosome assembly
explain: Dense staining, late replication, and gene scarcity are hallmarks of heterochromatin, and centromeric regions remain condensed in essentially all cell types, which is the constitutive form. Euchromatin is loosely packed, gene-rich, and transcriptionally active, the opposite of what is described. Facultative heterochromatin is condensed only in some cells or stages, such as the inactivated X, rather than constantly at the centromere. Naked DNA without nucleosomes is not what a stained chromosomal region represents.
hint: Decide first whether this is open or condensed chromatin, then ask whether centromeric condensation is the same in every cell type or context-dependent.

q: Bacterial cells lack histones, yet their single circular chromosome fits inside a cell far smaller than its contour length. How is bacterial DNA compacted?
options: By wrapping around histone octamers to form nucleosomes
By organization into supercoiled loops held by proteins, forming the nucleoid
By integrating into the plasma membrane as a single extended fiber
By converting entirely to the A-form, which is shorter than B-form
explain: Bacteria condense their circular chromosome into a series of negatively supercoiled loops anchored by DNA-binding proteins, producing the structure seen as the nucleoid. They do not build nucleosomes, since they lack the histone octamer. The chromosome is not simply embedded in the membrane as one long fiber, and shifting helical form does not provide the dramatic compaction needed. Supercoiled looping is the mechanism that achieves the necessary packing without histones.
hint: Without histones, what alternative twisting-and-looping strategy could shrink a circular genome, and what is the resulting body called?

q: Two genetically identical mice differ in coat color, and the difference is inherited yet correlates with the DNA methylation status of a coat-color locus rather than any change in the DNA sequence. This best illustrates which phenomenon?
options: A new point mutation in the coat-color gene
An epigenetic effect that is heritable and reversible without altering base sequence
A change in chromosome number affecting pigmentation
A supercoiling defect preventing transcription of the locus
explain: A heritable phenotypic difference that tracks with methylation but leaves the base sequence unchanged is the defining feature of an epigenetic effect, which can be passed on yet remains reversible. A point mutation would alter the actual sequence, which is explicitly excluded here. Differing chromosome number would be an aneuploidy affecting far more than pigmentation and is not implied. Supercoiling describes torsional state of the duplex and does not account for a methylation-linked, heritable coat-color difference.
hint: The animals share the same sequence but differ in a chemical tag on the DNA that offspring can inherit; what category of change is that?

q: A researcher transfers a segment of DNA from the centromeric region of one human chromosome to an ectopic (non-centromeric) location on another chromosome. Even though the original DNA sequence is present, a functional kinetochore fails to assemble at the new site, while the donor region loses centromere activity. What does this result best illustrate?
options: Centromere identity is specified epigenetically rather than by a fixed DNA sequence
Centromeric DNA must be methylated at every cytosine to recruit spindle fibers
The kinetochore binds only DNA sequences that are perfect palindromes
A single conserved consensus sequence determines centromere placement in all eukaryotes
explain: Centromeres are defined by an inherited chromatin state, chiefly the presence of the histone H3 variant CENP-A, not by a universal underlying sequence. Moving the DNA without its chromatin mark fails to seed a new centromere, demonstrating epigenetic specification. There is no single conserved consensus sequence across eukaryotes, centromere function does not depend on the DNA being palindromic, and blanket cytosine methylation is not the trigger for kinetochore assembly.
hint: Ask yourself what travels with the chromatin besides the bare nucleotide sequence, and whether the same DNA letters guarantee the same function in a new spot.

q: Human telomeres consist of many tandem copies of the sequence 5'-TTAGGG-3' and terminate in a single-stranded 3' overhang. Which feature of the telomere most directly prevents the chromosome end from being processed as a double-strand break?
options: The high density of CpG dinucleotides that silences nearby genes
A protein complex that caps the end and tucks the overhang into a protective loop
The presence of CENP-A nucleosomes that recruit the kinetochore
Continuous transcription of the repeat into a stable mRNA
explain: Shelterin proteins bind the telomeric repeats and the G-rich overhang, folding the end into a t-loop that hides it from the DNA-damage machinery so it is not seen as a broken end or fused to another chromosome. CENP-A belongs to centromeres, not telomeres. CpG silencing concerns gene regulation, not end protection, and telomeric repeats are largely not transcribed into a protective mRNA.
hint: Think about what physically hides the free end so repair enzymes don't 'see' it, and how a loop might bury an overhang.

q: Two amphibian species are closely related and similar in anatomical and developmental complexity, yet one has roughly fifteen times more nuclear DNA per cell than the other. This observation is the classic basis for which concept?
options: The central dogma of molecular biology
Chargaff's rule of base pairing
The C-value paradox
Mendel's law of independent assortment
explain: The C-value paradox names the puzzling lack of correlation between genome size (DNA per cell) and organismal complexity, exactly the situation where similar organisms differ enormously in DNA content, largely due to differing amounts of noncoding and repetitive DNA. The central dogma describes information flow, Chargaff's rule concerns equal amounts of complementary bases, and independent assortment is a rule of allele segregation, none of which address genome-size discrepancies.
hint: Focus on the mismatch between how much DNA a cell holds and how complicated the organism is.

q: A geneticist isolates a ~300 bp element that is present in over a million dispersed copies scattered throughout the human genome and is a remnant of a transposable element that moves via an RNA intermediate. Into which sequence class and category does this element best fit?
options: Unique-sequence DNA encoding a single-copy enzyme
Highly repetitive tandem DNA clustered at the centromere
A moderately repetitive interspersed SINE
A protein-coding member of a small gene family
explain: A short (~300 bp) element dispersed in many copies that derives from a retrotransposon is a SINE, the best-known example being Alu, which belongs to the moderately repetitive, interspersed class. Unique-sequence DNA exists in one or a few copies, so it does not fit a million-copy element. Highly repetitive satellite DNA is arranged in tandem at centromeres and telomeres, not interspersed. A gene-family member is a coding unique-sequence element, not a mobile interspersed repeat.
hint: A short element scattered widely and copied through an RNA intermediate points to one named family of interspersed repeats.

q: The human beta-globin locus contains several related genes (such as those expressed at different developmental stages) arrayed near one another, thought to have arisen by duplication of an ancestral gene. This cluster is best described as which of the following?
options: A gene family within the unique-sequence class of DNA
A block of highly repetitive satellite DNA
A cluster of identical tRNA genes in the moderately repetitive class
An array of LINE elements interspersed across a chromosome
explain: Related genes produced by duplication and divergence form a gene family, and protein-coding genes (present in one or a few copies) are part of the unique-sequence class. Satellite DNA is noncoding tandem repeats at centromeres/telomeres, not coding globin genes. tRNA genes are a different moderately repetitive set unrelated to globins, and LINEs are mobile interspersed repeats, not a coordinated set of developmentally expressed genes.
hint: Consider how a set of similar but distinct protein-coding genes arises and which broad sequence class houses coding genes.

q: During mitosis, spindle microtubules must attach to each replicated chromosome so the sister chromatids can be pulled to opposite poles. At which chromosomal structure does the protein platform for this attachment assemble, and what is its typical chromatin state?
options: At the telomere, within transcriptionally active euchromatin
At the centromere, within condensed heterochromatin
At an origin of replication, within open chromatin
Throughout the chromosome arms, evenly along euchromatin
explain: The kinetochore, the protein complex that anchors spindle microtubules, assembles at the centromere, which is embedded in densely packed heterochromatin. Telomeres are the protected ends and do not serve as microtubule attachment sites. Origins of replication initiate DNA synthesis but do not capture spindle fibers, and microtubule attachment is localized to the centromere rather than spread along the arms.
hint: Recall the constricted region where sisters stay joined and where the pulling apparatus grabs on, and whether that region is loosely or tightly packed.

q: A scientist performs a renaturation (reassociation) experiment on sheared genomic DNA and finds three kinetic fractions: one reassociates almost instantly, one at an intermediate rate, and one very slowly. The fastest-reassociating fraction corresponds to very short sequences tandemly repeated millions of times and is found concentrated near centromeres. Which class does this fastest fraction represent?
options: Unique-sequence DNA, including most protein-coding genes
Moderately repetitive DNA, such as rRNA gene clusters
Highly repetitive DNA, such as centromeric satellite repeats
Single-copy regulatory sequences scattered between genes
explain: The most abundant repeats reassociate fastest because complementary strands find partners quickly, and the fastest fraction here, very short tandem repeats in millions of copies near centromeres, is highly repetitive satellite DNA. Unique-sequence DNA reassociates slowest because each strand has only one partner. Moderately repetitive DNA reassociates at an intermediate rate. Single-copy regulatory sequences are also unique-sequence and would not dominate the instantaneous fraction.
hint: More copies mean complementary strands meet faster, so connect copy number to how quickly a fraction snaps back together.

q: A cancer cell line shows no progressive shortening of its chromosome ends across many divisions, unlike normal somatic cells. Reactivation of which activity most directly explains the maintained length of the G-rich terminal repeats?
options: An enzyme that extends the telomeric repeat array using an internal RNA template
A nuclease that trims the 3' overhang to expose blunt ends
A methyltransferase that adds methyl groups across centromeric satellite DNA
A topoisomerase that relaxes supercoils ahead of the replication fork
explain: Telomerase counteracts end-replication loss by adding TTAGGG repeats onto the 3' end using its own RNA as a template, so its reactivation keeps telomeres from shortening in immortalized cells. A nuclease trimming the overhang would shorten, not lengthen, the end. Centromeric methylation does not affect telomere length, and a topoisomerase relieves torsional stress during replication without adding terminal repeats.
hint: Think about what could rebuild the very ends each division and what unusual built-in component such an enzyme would carry to copy the right repeat.

q: A biochemist proposes a replication model in which the original double helix remains completely unaltered while a brand-new double helix is assembled alongside it. Which model does this description match?
options: Conservative replication
Semiconservative replication
Dispersive replication
Recombinational replication
explain: Keeping the entire parental duplex intact while producing a wholly new duplex is the defining feature of the model in which nothing old and new ends up mixed within a single helix. The model where strands separate and each templates a partner produces hybrids of one old and one new strand, so it is excluded. The model in which strands are broken into pieces yields helices that are patchworks of old and new segments, which contradicts an intact parental duplex. Recombinational replication is not one of the three classic models proposed for this question.
hint: Ask what happens to the original helix: is it preserved untouched, or does it contribute strands to the products?

q: In the model where each product double helix consists of exactly one parental strand paired with one newly synthesized strand, what is the underlying requirement at the start of each replication cycle?
options: The two parental strands must remain base-paired throughout synthesis
The two parental strands must separate so each can serve as a template
Both parental strands must be fragmented before copying begins
One parental strand must be degraded so only the other is copied
explain: Producing helices that each contain one old and one new strand requires that the parental strands come apart so that each lone strand directs synthesis of a complementary partner. Keeping the strands paired the whole time would prevent either from acting as a template. Fragmenting both strands describes a different model that yields mosaic products, not clean old-plus-new hybrids. Degrading one strand is unnecessary and would waste genetic information that must be conserved.
hint: For a single strand to be copied, what has to happen to its complementary partner first?

q: Researchers labeled bacterial DNA so that parental strands were dense and newly made strands were light, then used buoyant-density gradient centrifugation. After a single complete round of replication, every DNA molecule banded at one intermediate density. Which model is directly eliminated by this single result?
options: Conservative replication
Semiconservative replication
Dispersive replication
All three remain equally consistent
explain: A model predicting an intact heavy parental duplex plus a separate all-light new duplex would produce two distinct bands after one round, so observing only one band of intermediate density rules it out. The hybrid-strand model predicts exactly one intermediate band after one round, so it is consistent, not eliminated. The fragment-mosaic model also predicts a single intermediate band at this stage, so it too survives this particular result. Therefore not all models remain consistent.
hint: Which model would force two separate bands after one round, and does the data show two bands?

q: After a SECOND round of replication in the heavy-to-light labeling experiment, the DNA resolved into two bands: one of intermediate density and one of fully light density. Which conclusion is best supported?
options: The data fit the fragment-mosaic (dispersive) model and exclude the hybrid-strand model
The data fit the hybrid-strand (semiconservative) model and exclude the fragment-mosaic model
Both remaining models are equally supported by this outcome
The data require the fully conserved parental duplex model
explain: The hybrid-strand model predicts that after two rounds the population splits into half-intermediate and half-fully-light molecules, matching the two observed bands. The fragment-mosaic model predicts a single band that creeps toward lighter density and never separates into two distinct bands, so the appearance of two bands excludes it. Because one model matches and the other fails, the outcome does not support both equally. The fully conserved model was already excluded by the single intermediate band seen one round earlier.
hint: One model keeps everything in a single shifting band; the other splits into two distinct bands. Which did the gradient show?

q: Suppose dispersive (fragment-mosaic) replication were actually true. What density pattern would you expect after one round of replication starting from uniformly heavy DNA placed into light precursors?
options: One heavy band and one light band
A single band of intermediate density
Two bands, one intermediate and one light
A single fully light band
explain: If old and new material were intermixed in short patches along every strand, each molecule after one round would carry roughly half old and half new material distributed throughout, giving a single uniform band of intermediate density. Two separate heavy and light bands would arise only if old material stayed wholly segregated from new, which this model forbids. A split into intermediate and light bands does not appear until a later round under a different model. A fully light band would require no parental material at all, which is impossible after just one round.
hint: If old and new bits are scattered evenly along every molecule, how many distinct average densities exist after one copy?

q: The key insight that allowed the heavy-versus-light DNA classic experiment to distinguish replication models was the ability to:
options: Visualize individual replication forks under an electron microscope
Separate molecules by small differences in buoyant density during centrifugation
Sequence the order of bases in parental and daughter strands
Measure the rate of nucleotide incorporation with radioactive tracers
explain: Distinguishing the models hinged on physically resolving DNA molecules that differed only slightly in mass per volume, which a cesium-salt equilibrium gradient accomplishes by letting molecules settle at their matching density. Imaging replication forks reveals structure but cannot quantify the old-to-new ratio that separates the models. Reading base order does not report on which strands are old versus new. Tracking incorporation rate measures speed, not the density distribution that discriminates among the three predicted band patterns.
hint: What physical property of the molecules was being measured, and what technique sorts molecules by that property?

q: A geneticist replicates uniformly heavy DNA in light precursors and lets it go through THREE rounds of replication. Under semiconservative replication, what fraction of the resulting double helices will be of intermediate (hybrid) density?
options: One half
One quarter
One eighth
Zero
explain: Each of the two original heavy strands is conserved and stays paired with a newer light strand, so the number of hybrid molecules is fixed at two no matter how many rounds occur. Each round doubles the total population: one molecule becomes two, then four, then eight after three rounds. With two hybrids among eight total molecules, the hybrid fraction is two-eighths, which reduces to a quarter. A half describes the population right after a single round, when both molecules are hybrid (two of two). After two rounds there are four molecules with two still hybrid, giving a half again. A hybrid fraction of zero is impossible because the parental strands are never destroyed and continue to form hybrids indefinitely.
hint: Count how many original template strands survive, then divide by the total number of molecules after three doublings.

q: During the labeling experiment, why was it essential to grow the bacteria for MANY generations in the heavy-isotope medium BEFORE switching them to the light medium?
options: To ensure essentially all DNA strands were uniformly heavy at the experiment's start
To increase the bacterial population to a countable size
To force the cells to pause replication until the switch
To convert the heavy isotope into a radioactive form for detection
explain: A clean starting point required that the parental DNA be fully and uniformly dense, which is achieved only after enough generations for the heavy isotope to be incorporated into virtually every strand. Simply growing more cells would not guarantee uniform labeling of the DNA itself. The cells were never made to stop replicating; continuous division is what produced the labeled DNA. The heavy isotope used was nonradioactive and distinguished by mass alone, so no conversion to a radioactive form occurred.
hint: Think about what the density baseline must look like before the switch for the later bands to be interpretable.

q: A geneticist defines a particular stretch of DNA as 'all the sequence whose copying is directed by a single start site.' Which term matches this definition?
options: A replicon
A replication fork
A telomere
A centromere
explain: The unit of DNA replicated from one origin of replication is the entity being described, since it is the span of sequence whose duplication is controlled by that single start site. The point where the parental strands split apart is a moving junction, not a span of sequence. A chromosome-end structure protects the tip of a linear molecule but is not defined by an origin. A constriction region serves spindle attachment during division and has nothing to do with delimiting a unit of copying.
hint: Think about what unit of DNA is delimited by exactly one place where copying begins.

q: A circular bacterial chromosome is observed mid-replication. From a single origin, two forks travel in opposite directions and eventually meet on the opposite side. Compared with a version where copying proceeds from the same origin in only one direction, the bidirectional case will:
options: Finish copying the chromosome in roughly half the time
Require twice as many origins to complete
Produce linear rather than circular products
Yield four daughter molecules instead of two
explain: With two forks each handling about half the circle, the genome is duplicated in approximately half the time needed when a lone fork must traverse the entire molecule. The number of origins is unchanged; both scenarios start from one origin. The template is a closed circle in both cases, so the products remain circular regardless of fork number. One round of copying a single circle yields two daughter molecules whether one fork or two are used.
hint: Count how much of the molecule each fork must cover when there are two versus one.

q: Under the electron microscope, a replicating circular molecule shows a closed loop with an internal bubble that gives it the shape of a familiar Greek letter. What does the bubble region directly represent?
options: The portion already separated into single-stranded templates and being copied
The single origin sequence in its unwound resting state
The two finished circular daughter molecules
The site where a fork has stalled permanently
explain: The internal loop is the stretch where the parental duplex has been opened into templates and new strands are being made, expanding outward from the origin. The origin is merely the start point within the bubble, not the whole bubble. Finished daughter circles appear only after the structure resolves, not during this intermediate. A stalled fork would not by itself generate the characteristic expanding-bubble appearance of normal progress.
hint: The opened-up loop corresponds to template that has already been separated; ask what is happening there.

q: Consider a junction in a replicating molecule where the double-stranded parent has just come apart into two exposed single strands that serve as templates. This precise junction is best called:
options: A replication fork
An origin of replication
A replicon
A replication bubble
explain: The moving point at which the parental duplex splits into two template strands is exactly the structure in question. The start site where unwinding first begins is a fixed sequence, not the moving splitting junction. The whole span copied from one origin is a larger unit, not a single junction. The opened loop is the expanded region as a whole, bounded by such junctions rather than being one itself.
hint: Name the moving point, not the region it bounds or the place it started.

q: A human cell must duplicate roughly 6 billion base pairs during S phase, yet it finishes in only several hours. Which feature most directly explains how such a large genome is copied so quickly?
options: Copying initiates from many origins along each chromosome at once
Each chromosome relies on a single highly processive fork
Forks move thousands of times faster than in bacteria
The genome is circular, allowing theta-style copying
explain: Spacing thousands of origins across the chromosomes lets many segments be copied simultaneously, so the total time is set by the longest stretch between adjacent starts rather than the whole genome length. A single fork per chromosome would take far too long for a genome this size. Eukaryotic fork speed is actually slower than bacterial, so raw fork velocity is not the explanation. Linear eukaryotic chromosomes are not circles and do not use theta intermediates.
hint: How can a huge molecule be finished fast if many regions are worked on in parallel?

q: On a long linear eukaryotic chromosome, two neighboring origins each fire and produce expanding bubbles. As replication continues along that region, what happens to the two bubbles?
options: They expand toward each other and fuse into one continuous replicated stretch
They repel each other and leave a permanent unreplicated gap
They merge their origins into a single new origin
They collapse back to the duplex and refire from scratch
explain: As each bubble grows, its forks advance until the forks of adjacent bubbles meet and the replicated regions join into a continuous copied segment. Bubbles do not push apart, and leaving a gap would mean incomplete duplication. Origins are sequence elements that do not physically combine into one. Normal progress does not reverse and restart; the bubbles simply expand and coalesce.
hint: Picture two growing bubbles on the same fiber and follow their edges as they advance.

q: Using thousands of origins lets a eukaryotic cell replicate its genome rapidly, but this strategy creates a regulatory demand that single-origin bacteria face far less acutely. What is that demand?
options: Ensuring each origin is used exactly once per cell cycle
Choosing which one origin will fire in a given division
Preventing the chromosome from becoming circular
Keeping all origins permanently active between divisions
explain: With many start sites, the cell must license each one so it fires a single time per cycle; otherwise some regions would be over-replicated, producing extra copies. Picking among origins is not the core issue when thousands fire each cycle. Eukaryotic chromosomes are linear and there is no tendency to circularize that origins must counter. Origins should not stay continuously active; firing is restricted to the appropriate phase, which is the opposite of the proposed alternative.
hint: If many starts exist, what bookkeeping problem arises about how often each one is allowed to go?

q: A student studies a closed circular plasmid and finds that, throughout S-phase-equivalent copying, replication always proceeds away from one fixed site in a single direction with just one moving fork, all the way around until it returns to the start. Which description best fits this plasmid's replication?
options: Unidirectional theta replication from a single origin
Bidirectional theta replication from a single origin
Multi-origin replication like a eukaryotic chromosome
Bubble fusion between several adjacent origins
explain: A single fork advancing one way around a circle from one start point describes a one-directional theta mechanism on a single origin. A two-fork pattern moving in opposite directions would be the two-directional variant, which contradicts the single fork observed. Multiple origins are a feature of large linear chromosomes, not this single-start circle. Fusion of adjacent bubbles requires several origins, which this molecule does not have.
hint: One start, one fork, one direction around a circle — match it to the right theta variant.

q: During DNA replication, an incoming nucleotide is joined to the growing strand by a covalent bond. Which chemical event drives this addition and which groups react?
options: The 3'-hydroxyl of the growing strand attacks the innermost (alpha) phosphate of the incoming nucleotide, releasing pyrophosphate
The 5'-phosphate of the growing strand attacks the 3'-hydroxyl of the incoming nucleotide, releasing a single phosphate
The 3'-hydroxyl of the incoming nucleotide attacks the 5'-phosphate of the growing strand, releasing water
The 5'-hydroxyl of the growing strand attacks the alpha phosphate of the incoming nucleotide, releasing pyrophosphate
explain: Each new nucleotide arrives as a triphosphate, and the free 3'-hydroxyl at the end of the growing chain acts as the nucleophile that attacks the phosphate closest to the sugar of the incoming nucleotide. This forms the new phosphodiester bond and cleaves off the two outer phosphates as pyrophosphate, whose later hydrolysis makes the reaction energetically favorable. The reactive end that grows must be the 3'-hydroxyl, never the 5'-phosphate, so any scheme placing the phosphate end as the attacker or releasing only water or a single phosphate misrepresents the chemistry.
hint: Which end of the chain stays reactive, and what part of the triphosphate substrate is left behind when the bond forms?

q: A student claims that one new strand at a replication fork is built 5'->3' while the other is built 3'->5'. Why is this claim incorrect?
options: DNA polymerase can only extend a chain by adding to a free 3'-hydroxyl, so every strand is built 5'->3'
DNA polymerase reads template in both directions, so strand polarity is irrelevant
Only the leading strand obeys directionality rules; the lagging strand is built 3'->5'
Both strands are actually built 3'->5' because the template is read 5'->3'
explain: The enzyme adds nucleotides exclusively to the exposed 3'-hydroxyl of the chain, so the new strand can only elongate in the 5'->3' direction no matter which template it copies. The illusion that one strand 'goes backward' comes from the antiparallel templates pointing in opposite directions, but both new strands are still synthesized 5'->3'. The lagging strand is no exception; it simply achieves the overall opposite orientation through many short pieces, not by reversing the chemistry.
hint: Think about the single chemical constraint of the polymerase rather than how the fork looks overall.

q: At a single replication fork moving left to right, the leading strand is being made continuously toward the right. What is true of the lagging strand at this same fork?
options: Its Okazaki fragments are each elongated leftward, opposite to fork movement, then joined together
It is synthesized continuously toward the right, just slower than the leading strand
It is synthesized continuously toward the left as one piece using a single primer
It is built rightward in fragments that each require no primer
explain: Because the two templates are antiparallel, the strand whose template runs the 'wrong' way cannot be extended toward the moving fork; instead the polymerase repeatedly starts behind the fork and elongates each short fragment away from the fork's direction of travel. As the fork opens more template, a new fragment begins, so the net growth points opposite to fork motion and the pieces are later sealed into one strand. A continuous single-primer synthesis or a primer-free mechanism would violate the polymerase's need for a 3' end and a fresh primer on each fragment.
hint: Relate the direction each short piece grows to the way the fork is opening, given antiparallel templates.

q: Why does the lagging strand require a new primer for each segment it makes, whereas the leading strand generally needs only one primer at the start?
options: Each Okazaki fragment is a fresh start that has no upstream 3'-OH to extend, so it needs its own primer
The lagging strand uses a different polymerase that cannot recognize existing primers
Primers on the lagging strand are destroyed by the fork before they can be reused
The leading strand never uses a primer, so only the lagging strand consumes any primers
explain: A polymerase can only add onto an existing 3'-hydroxyl, so every time synthesis must restart behind the advancing fork there is no prior strand end to grab, and a new primer must be laid down to provide that 3' end. The leading strand, by contrast, is extended continuously from one starting primer because its growth follows the fork without interruption. The leading strand does in fact begin with a primer, and the repeated priming on the lagging strand reflects its discontinuous restarts, not a separate enzyme or primer destruction.
hint: Consider what a polymerase needs before it can add even a single nucleotide, and how often the lagging strand restarts.

q: A researcher isolates newly made short DNA pieces of roughly 1500 nucleotides each from a replicating cell, then in another organism finds analogous pieces averaging about 150 nucleotides. What is the most reasonable interpretation?
options: The longer fragments are from a bacterium and the shorter from a eukaryote, both being lagging-strand intermediates
The longer fragments are leading-strand pieces and the shorter are lagging-strand pieces in the same cell
Both sizes come from the leading strand, which is cut into pieces during proofreading
The shorter fragments are from a bacterium and the longer from a eukaryote
explain: These short transient pieces are the hallmark of discontinuous lagging-strand synthesis, and their characteristic length differs by domain of life: bacterial fragments run on the order of one to two thousand nucleotides, while eukaryotic fragments are an order of magnitude smaller, around one to two hundred. So a population near 1500 fits a bacterium and one near 150 fits a eukaryote. The leading strand is made as one continuous piece and is not chopped up, so attributing the fragments to it is wrong, and the size assignment to the domains cannot be reversed.
hint: Recall which strand yields short transient pieces and how their typical length differs between bacteria and eukaryotes.

q: Which feature of the two template strands is the fundamental reason the new strands at one fork end up running in opposite directions?
options: The template strands are antiparallel, so copying each 5'->3' yields products pointing opposite ways
The template strands are parallel, forcing the polymerase to switch directions midway
The template strands have identical sequences, so synthesis mirrors them
The two templates are replicated by polymerases with opposite chemical polarity
explain: The two strands of the duplex run head-to-tail relative to each other, so when each is copied under the universal 5'->3' rule the resulting new strands necessarily point in opposite directions along the helix. This antiparallel arrangement, not any difference between polymerases, is what creates the leading/lagging asymmetry. Parallel or identical-sequence templates do not describe real DNA, and no polymerase reverses its chemical polarity to copy a strand.
hint: What geometric relationship between the two strands, combined with one fixed synthesis direction, produces opposite products?

q: Suppose a mutant polymerase loses its 5'->3' synthesis ability but a hypothetical enzyme could instead add nucleotides to a 5'-phosphate end. What problem would such 3'->5' growth face that the normal mechanism avoids?
options: The triphosphate energy needed for the next bond would sit on the growing strand's end, so a single proofreading removal would strip the activating group and stall elongation
It could not copy antiparallel templates at all, since only parallel templates allow growth from a 5' end
It would be unable to use any primer because primers only present 5' ends
It would synthesize twice as fast but could never form phosphodiester bonds
explain: In normal 5'->3' growth the high-energy triphosphate is carried on each incoming nucleotide, so if a mismatched base is excised the chain still retains a good 3'-OH ready to continue. If growth instead proceeded by adding to a 5' end, the activating triphosphate would reside on the terminal nucleotide of the chain itself, and removing a wrong base during proofreading would also remove the energy source needed to add the next one, stalling synthesis. This is a real rationale for the universal directionality; the other statements misstate template geometry, primer ends, or invent contradictory claims about bond formation.
hint: Think about where the bond's energy is stored in each scheme, and what happens to that energy if the last nucleotide must be removed.

q: A linear template is being replicated and you label the leading-strand product and the lagging-strand product separately at a single fork. Compared with the leading strand, what will the lagging-strand product look like before final processing?
options: A series of separate short stretches each begun from its own primer, not yet sealed into one strand
One unbroken strand identical in continuity to the leading strand
A single long stretch with a single primer at its 3' end
A continuous strand that grows in the same direction as the fork moves
explain: Before the joining step, the lagging strand exists as a collection of discrete short pieces, each one initiated from its own primer behind the fork, which is exactly what makes its synthesis discontinuous. Only after these pieces are processed and ligated does it become one continuous strand resembling the leading strand. Describing it as already continuous, single-primed, or growing in the fork's direction contradicts the discontinuous, away-from-fork nature of its assembly.
hint: Picture the lagging strand mid-replication, before the sealing step that comes later.

q: During bacterial DNA replication, a researcher isolates a mutant cell in which the enzyme that synthesizes RNA primers is non-functional. Which immediate consequence is most likely?
options: DNA polymerase III cannot begin synthesizing new strands because it lacks a free 3'-OH to extend
The replication fork over-rotates and accumulates positive supercoils ahead of itself
Single-stranded regions collapse into hairpin loops that block the helicase
The two parental strands fail to separate at the origin of replication
explain: Replicative DNA polymerases can only add nucleotides to an existing 3'-hydroxyl group; they cannot initiate a strand de novo. The enzyme that lays down short RNA primers supplies that starting 3'-OH, so losing it stalls new strand synthesis directly. Supercoiling buildup is the job of a topoisomerase and is unrelated to priming. Hairpin prevention is handled by strand-coating proteins, and strand separation at the origin depends on an initiator protein and helicase, none of which require the primer-making enzyme to function.
hint: Think about what a DNA polymerase physically requires before it can add even one nucleotide.

q: Which statement best distinguishes the roles of DNA polymerase I and DNA polymerase III in bacteria?
options: Polymerase III is the main elongating enzyme, while polymerase I removes RNA primers and fills the resulting gaps with DNA
Polymerase I is the main elongating enzyme, while polymerase III only proofreads finished strands
Both enzymes synthesize DNA but only polymerase III can remove RNA primers
Polymerase I unwinds the helix while polymerase III seals nicks between fragments
explain: The high-processivity polymerase carries out the bulk of strand elongation on both leading and lagging templates, whereas the other polymerase uses a 5'->3' exonuclease activity to excise RNA primers and replace them with DNA. The main elongating enzyme does not remove primers, unwinding is performed by a helicase, and sealing the final nick is the job of a ligase, so the remaining descriptions misassign these functions.
hint: Consider which enzyme handles the heavy lifting of elongation and which one cleans up the RNA left behind.

q: As a replication fork advances, the parental duplex ahead of it becomes increasingly overwound. Which enzyme relieves this strain, and to what general class does it belong?
options: DNA gyrase, a type II topoisomerase that introduces transient breaks to relax the supercoils
DNA helicase, a motor protein that adds nucleotides at the break point
Primase, an RNA polymerase that nicks and rejoins the backbone
Single-strand-binding protein, a structural protein that rotates the helix
explain: Torsional stress generated ahead of the fork is relieved by a type II topoisomerase that transiently cuts both strands, allows the tension to dissipate, and reseals them. A helicase only breaks hydrogen bonds and does not add nucleotides; the primer-making enzyme synthesizes RNA and does not manage supercoiling; and the strand-coating proteins merely stabilize single strands without altering topology.
hint: Picture twisting a rope from one end while holding the other fixed - what kind of enzyme could release that twist?
